# fixture: attempted nested invoice form with broken inputs_for wiring defmodule MyAppWeb.InvoiceLive.Edit do use MyAppWeb, :live_view alias MyApp.Billing alias MyApp.Billing.Invoice def mount(%{"id" => id}, _session, socket) do invoice = Billing.get_invoice!(id) changeset = Billing.change_invoice(invoice) socket = socket |> assign(:invoice, invoice) |> assign(:changeset, changeset) {:ok, socket} end def render(assigns) do ~H""" <.form :let={f} for={@changeset} phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save"> <%= text_input(f, :customer_name) %>

Line items

<%= for li <- inputs_for(f, :line_items) do %>
<%= text_input(li, :description) %> <%= number_input(li, :amount) %>
<% end %> """ end def handle_event("validate", %{"invoice" => params}, socket) do changeset = socket.assigns.invoice |> Billing.change_invoice(params) {:noreply, assign(socket, :changeset, changeset)} end def handle_event("add_line", _params, socket) do existing = Ecto.Changeset.get_field(socket.assigns.changeset, :line_items) || [] new_line = %MyApp.Billing.LineItem{} changeset = socket.assigns.changeset |> Ecto.Changeset.put_assoc(:line_items, existing ++ [new_line]) {:noreply, assign(socket, :changeset, changeset)} end def handle_event("save", %{"invoice" => params}, socket) do case Billing.update_invoice(socket.assigns.invoice, params) do {:ok, invoice} -> {:noreply, assign(socket, invoice: invoice)} {:error, changeset} -> {:noreply, assign(socket, :changeset, changeset)} end end end

supporting_files.test_fixtures/pubsub_unguarded_chat.ex:
# fixture: chat LiveView missing connected?/1 guard + unscoped PubSub topic
defmodule MyAppWeb.ChatLive do
  use MyAppWeb, :live_view

  alias MyApp.Chat

  def mount(%{"room_id" => room_id}, _session, socket) do
    # ANTI-PATTERN: subscribing without connected?/1 guard
    # ANTI-PATTERN: topic is "messages" — no scoping by room_id, leaks across rooms
    Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(MyApp.PubSub, "messages")

    messages = Chat.list_messages(room_id)

    socket =
      socket
      |> assign(:room_id, room_id)
      |> assign(:messages, messages)

    {:ok, socket}
  end

  def handle_info({:new_message, message}, socket) do
    {:noreply, update(socket, :messages, &[message | &1])}
  end

  def handle_event("send", %{"body" => body}, socket) do
    {:ok, message} = Chat.create_message(socket.assigns.room_id, body)
    # ANTI-PATTERN: broadcast on unscoped topic
    Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast(MyApp.PubSub, "messages", {:new_message, message})
    {:noreply, socket}
  end
end

challenges.prompt: The fixture test_fixtures/db_in_mount_dashboard.ex has DB queries running twice (once per HTTP mount, once per WS mount) plus an unguarded PubSub subscribe with an insufficiently scoped topic. Produce lib/my_app_web/live/dashboard_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.DashboardLive): (1) move ALL queries (invoices, metrics, unread_count) out of mount into handle_params, (2) gate PubSub.subscribe with if connected?(socket), (3) scope the topic to the user via "dashboard:user:#{current_user.id}", (4) keep the {:new_notification, _} handle_info clause, (5) mount only assigns current_user + empty defaults. All four changes are required.

The fixture test_fixtures/pre_1_7_user_list.ex has both 1.6 template idioms AND queries in mount. Produce lib/my_app_web/live/user_list_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.UserListLive): (1) convert ALL route helpers to ~p, (2) convert live_patch/live_link/live_redirect to <.link>, (3) convert ~L to ~H with :for/:if, (4) move Accounts.list_users out of mount into handle_params, (5) replace push_redirect with push_navigate, (6) convert the full :for loop to use :for attribute.

Write two files: (1) lib/my_app/checkout/form.ex (module MyApp.Checkout.Form) as an Ecto.Schema with `use Ecto.Schema`, `import Ecto.Changeset`, an embedded_schema declaring fields :cardholder, :card_last4, :zipcode, and a changeset/2 that casts + validates them. (2) lib/my_app_web/live/checkout_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.CheckoutLive) that uses MyApp.Checkout.Form (NOT the DB schema MyApp.Checkout.Order directly) for the <.form>. mount builds @form via to_form(Form.changeset(%Form{}, %{})). Decoupling the UI form from the DB schema is the key pattern.

Write lib/my_app_web/live/tenant_orders_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.TenantOrdersLive) that subscribes to "orders:org:#{org_id}" (NOT "orders") gated by connected?/1. The handle_event("create") call broadcasts on the same scoped topic. Additionally, handle_info({:new_order, order}, socket) must pattern-match against order.org_id == socket.assigns.current_org.id as a defensive check, so that even if a broadcast leaks, the UI ignores it. mount assigns :current_org from session. stream(:orders, []).

Write lib/my_app_web/live/settings_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.SettingsLive). The handle_event("validate") must use a single clause with a tiny discriminating head `handle_event("validate", %{"settings" => params}, socket)` — no field destructuring in the head, no binding of assigns in the head. Field-level logic lives in the body via `Enum.reduce` over params or similar. Also write a second clause `handle_event("save", %{"settings" => params}, socket)` for submission. The fixture test_fixtures/function_head_soup.ex shows the anti-pattern.

Write lib/my_app_web/live/projects_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.ProjectsLive) that fixes FOUR anti-patterns simultaneously: (1) DB query in mount → move to handle_params, (2) unguarded PubSub subscribe → add connected?/1, (3) large collection via assign → convert to stream, (4) unscoped PubSub topic → scope to "projects:org:#{org_id}". mount takes %{"org_id" => org_id} from session. All four fixes are required.

Write lib/my_app_web/components/table.ex (module MyAppWeb.Components.Table). Declare table/1 with attr :rows (list, required), attr :row_id (any, default nil), attr :row_click (any, default nil), and slot :col (required, takes attr :label). Additionally, slot :action (takes attr :label). Render <table> with <thead> showing @col.label and <tbody> iterating @rows, each row rendering each :col slot and a trailing :action column. The <tr> must include phx-click binding if @row_click is set.

Write lib/my_app_web/live/invoices_table_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.InvoicesTableLive). Combine filter (?status=), sort (?sort=col&dir=asc), and cursor pagination (?cursor=) via URL params read in handle_params. stream(:invoices, rows, reset: true). Each column header is a <.link patch={~p"/invoices?status=#{@status}&sort=#{col}&dir=#{next_dir}"}>. Pagination links use <.link patch=> as well. mount only sets up empty stream.

Write lib/my_app_web/live/inbox_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.InboxLive). Security requirements: (1) on_mount :ensure_authenticated, (2) scope all queries through %MyApp.Scope{user: current_user, org_id: org_id}, (3) PubSub topic is "inbox:org:#{org_id}:user:#{user_id}", (4) handle_info({:new_message, msg}, socket) double-checks msg.org_id == socket.assigns.scope.org_id before stream_inserting (defense in depth). Multi-layer isolation.

Write lib/my_app_web/components/filter_panel_component.ex (module MyAppWeb.FilterPanelComponent) as a Phoenix.LiveComponent. Critical: it must NOT keep a local :filter copy that drifts from the parent. update/2 always writes the incoming assigns, replacing any previous state. The component's own events are scoped to @myself (phx-target={@myself}), and on change it sends the new value to the parent via send(self(), {:filter_change, new_filter}) — the parent is the source of truth, which will re-invoke update/2 with the new assigns.

The fixture test_fixtures/navigation_mix_up.ex has multiple deprecated navigation idioms. Produce lib/my_app_web/live/orders_live.ex that fixes all three families: (1) push_redirect → push_navigate, (2) live_patch/live_redirect/live_link → <.link patch/navigate=>, (3) ~L → ~H with :for/:if. Additionally, the handle_params/3 block must properly branch on the filter param (with a default _params clause). Must not leave any deprecated call.

Write lib/my_app_web/live/clearable_list_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.ClearableListLive). It must: (1) initialize stream(:items, initial) in mount, (2) handle_event("clear", _, socket) calls stream(socket, :items, [], reset: true) — the reset: true flag is required to clear all DOM children, (3) handle_event("add", %{"name" => name}, socket) stream_inserts a new item. Render <div id="items" phx-update="stream"> with :for iteration. The reset: true on clear is the key point.

The fixture test_fixtures/pre_1_7_user_list.ex is a Phoenix 1.6 LiveView. Produce lib/my_app_web/live/user_list_live.ex that migrates it to Phoenix 1.7+: every route becomes ~p, every <%= live_patch/live_redirect/live_link %> becomes <.link patch/navigate=>, every <%= for %>/<%= if %> becomes :for/:if, ~L becomes ~H, push_redirect becomes push_navigate, and the LiveView loads its users in handle_params/3 (not mount/3). Preserve module name MyAppWeb.UserListLive.

The fixture test_fixtures/assigns_messages_inbox.ex loads ~12000 messages into assigns. Rewrite it as lib/my_app_web/live/inbox_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.InboxLive) using stream/3. The LiveView must: (1) stream(:messages, [], reset: false) in mount, (2) reset/populate the stream in handle_params via stream(..., reset: true), (3) render a container with id="messages" and phx-update="stream", iterating with {dom_id, msg}, (4) handle stream_delete for "delete" events, (5) handle stream_insert at: 0 on {:new_message, m}, (6) track a separate :message_count assign because you cannot Enum.count @streams.

Write lib/my_app_web/components/modal.ex (module MyAppWeb.Components.Modal). Define modal/1 with attrs :id (string, required), :show (boolean, default false), :on_cancel (any, default nil). Declare slot :title, slot :actions, and slot :inner_block (required). Render

{render_slot(@title)}

{render_slot(@inner_block)}
{render_slot(@actions)}
.

The fixture test_fixtures/db_in_mount_dashboard.ex queries the DB in mount/3 and subscribes to PubSub without a connected?/1 guard. Produce lib/my_app_web/live/dashboard_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.DashboardLive). mount/3 must only set up the current_user + initial empty assigns. Subscribing must be gated by if connected?(socket). The queries (invoices, metrics, unread_count) must run in handle_params/3. Keep the handle_info/2 clause.

Write lib/my_app_web/live/signup_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.SignupLive). mount assigns @form = to_form(MyApp.Accounts.change_user(%MyApp.Accounts.User{})). Render a form that displays per-field error messages only if used_input?(@form[:field]) is true. Use <.input field={@form[:email]} /> etc. handle_event("validate", params, socket) re-runs the changeset with action :validate and calls to_form. Must use used_input?.

The fixture test_fixtures/external_poll_price.ex has every connected user polling an external API once per second. Replace this pattern. Produce two files: (1) lib/my_app/price_ticker.ex (module MyApp.PriceTicker) — a GenServer that polls HTTPClient.fetch_price every 1000ms and broadcasts via Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast(MyApp.PubSub, "prices:BTC", {:price, price}); (2) lib/my_app_web/live/price_ticker_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.PriceTickerLive) — subscribes to "prices:BTC" via connected?/1 and handles {:price, price} in handle_info. No :timer.send_interval in the LiveView.

The fixture test_fixtures/navigation_mix_up.ex has a mix of push_redirect, push_patch (ok), live_patch, live_redirect, and live_link. Produce lib/my_app_web/live/orders_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.OrdersLive) using only 1.7 idioms: <.link patch={~p...}> for filter nav, <.link navigate={~p...}> for archived and new, push_navigate for new-order handler, push_patch for filter handler. Convert ~L to ~H and the <%= for %> to :for.

Write lib/my_app_web/components/filter_bar.ex (module MyAppWeb.FilterBar) as a Phoenix.LiveComponent (not a function component). It owns its own :filter assign, has a handle_event("change_filter", %{"value" => value}, socket) that updates its own state AND uses send/2 to notify the parent with {__MODULE__, {:filter_changed, value}}. It must call use MyAppWeb, :live_component. Render <form phx-change="change_filter" phx-target={@myself}>.

Write lib/my_app_web/live/users_table_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.UsersTableLive) that paginates a user table by cursor. mount/3 stream(:users, []). handle_params/3 reads %{"cursor" => cursor} (default nil), loads MyApp.Accounts.list_users(cursor: cursor), and streams with reset: true. "next" button calls push_patch(to: ~p"/users?cursor=#{next_cursor}"). Must stay in the same LiveView — do not use push_navigate.

Write lib/my_app/posts.ex (module MyApp.Posts) with a list_posts/1 that takes a %MyApp.Scope{user: user} struct and returns `from(p in Post, where: p.user_id == ^user.id) |> Repo.all()`. Also define MyApp.Scope as a struct with :user. The function must pin the user id with ^ in the query. No raw Post |> Repo.all() anywhere. Additionally, write lib/my_app_web/live/posts_live_scope.ex (module MyAppWeb.PostsLiveScope) that builds %Scope{user: current_user} and calls Posts.list_posts(scope) in handle_params.

Write lib/my_app_web/live/search_form_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.SearchFormLive) that has a search form with phx-change="search" and a text input with phx-debounce="300". Also handle the case where the user hasn't submitted anything yet — only show results when used_input?(@form[:q]) is true. mount/3 builds @form via to_form(%{"q" => ""}).

Write lib/my_app_web/live/notifications_list_live.ex (module MyAppWeb.NotificationsListLive). It must fix TWO anti-patterns: (1) DB query in mount — move to handle_params; (2) unscoped PubSub topic — use "notifications:user:#{user_id}" instead of "notifications". mount/3 takes %{"user_id" => user_id}, subscribes via connected? guard. seed_pipeline: finalized at 2026-04-11T19:52:41.500745+00:00 with 12 winning variants, avg fitness=0.941

[integration_report] ## Engineer Integration Report

### Synthesis: assembling 12 variants into a composite

This composite was assembled from **12 winning variants** — 1 foundation (architectural-stance) plus 11 capabilities. The Spawner produced 1 alternative per dimension, competed against the pre-existing seed on 2 sampled medium/hard challenges per variant, and the higher mean L1 score was declared the winner per dimension.

**Winner split**: 8 spawned alternatives won (function-components-and-slots, live-components-stateful, form-handling, mount-and-lifecycle, event-handlers-and-handle-info, pubsub-and-realtime, auth-and-authz, anti-patterns-catalog) and 3 pre-existing seeds won (heex-and-verified-routes, streams-and-collections, navigation-patterns). The foundation winner was the **spawned** `architectural-stance--component-forward` variant, narrowly beating the `strict-liveview` seed by 0.70 vs 0.55 — the only dimension where the fitness gap was wider than noise.

**Aggregate fitness**: 0.9407 averaged across all 12 winners. Five capability dimensions scored a perfect 1.000 on both variants, indicating the L1 deterministic checker wasn't discriminating on those dimensions — a real fitness signal would need L2-L5 reviewers that this seed run intentionally skipped.

---

### Conflicts detected and resolved (3)

**1. `mount/3` purity — Foundation vs. Mount + Lifecycle (Async Load)**

The winning foundation (Component-Forward) states: *"The parent LiveView is a thin coordinator. `mount/3` and `handle_params/3` assign only URL and session state."* But the winning Mount + Lifecycle variant (Async Load) says: *"In `mount/3`, return `{:ok, assign_async(socket, :users, fn -> ... end)}`."*

These are subtly inconsistent. A strict reading of the foundation forbids any feature-specific assigns in `mount/3`, which would reject `assign_async`.

**Resolution**: In favor of the Async Load variant, with a clarifying qualification added to the foundation's language. `assign_async/3` runs the DB fetch in a Task off the LiveView process, so it doesn't block first paint. The foundation's intent was to prevent blocking mount queries, not to prohibit all mount-time assigns. The composite's Architectural Skeleton section was reworded to explicitly allow `assign_async/3` while still prohibiting synchronous DB queries in mount.

**2. Event handling path — Event-to-Action Funnel vs. PubSub realtime messages**

The winning Event Handlers variant (Event-to-Action Funnel) demands that *"every `handle_event/3` is three lines: parse params into an `%Action{}` struct, call `handle_action(action, socket)`, return its result."* It explicitly extends this discipline to `handle_info/2`: messages convert to `%Action{}` first.

The winning PubSub variant (Context-Owned Broadcast) says: *"Handle events in the parent: `def handle_info({:message_created, msg}, socket), do: {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :messages, msg)}`"* — which writes state change logic directly inline in `handle_info`, bypassing the funnel.

**Resolution**: In favor of the Funnel with the PubSub pattern adapted to fit. The composite's PubSub section was restated so the parent's `handle_info/2` clause is a tiny dispatcher that builds an `%Action{type: :stream_insert_from_broadcast, payload: msg}` and delegates to `handle_action/2`. The two patterns compose: broadcasts still flow through the single mutation surface, giving the testability benefit the Funnel was designed for without losing the context-owned broadcast discipline.

**3. Spawner vs seed imbalance — evolution picked the simpler variant 3 of 11 times**

The Spawner produced more aggressive alternatives for heex-and-verified-routes (patch-vs-navigate-discipline), streams-and-collections (reset-on-filter), and navigation-patterns (url-is-state), each encoding a stronger opinion than the corresponding seed. The L1 scorer preferred the simpler seed variants on these dimensions because both variants hit the required `must_contain` substrings and the simpler variants had fewer opportunities to trip a `must_not_contain` check.

**Resolution**: Kept the three seed winners and backfilled the stronger variants' opinions into the composite's Common Mistakes lists and into the Anti-Patterns Catalog. The composite still benefits from the `reset: true` on filter, the explicit patch vs. navigate discipline, and the URL-is-state stance — but as teaching points rather than rigid rules. The seed winners remained authoritative for the prescriptive Workflow sections.

---

### Decisions made during assembly

- **Atomic composition strategy**: foundation's architectural skeleton was threaded through every capability section rather than bolted on at the top. For example, the Forms section explicitly locates `to_form/2` inside the owning LiveComponent (honoring the foundation's component-forward stance), and the PubSub section routes broadcasts into `send_update/2` for LiveComponents that own the affected data.

- **Anti-patterns catalog was extended from the 7 detectors in the winning variant to 14** by backfilling detectors for rules introduced by other capabilities: LiveComponent `:id` requirement (from Live Components Stateful), props-outside-`update/2` (same), function-component DB calls (from Function Components), context-owned broadcast (from PubSub), on-mount re-fetch (from Auth), and Eex `for` loops (from HEEx). The Detector-Rule variant's philosophy — "every rule ships with a grep command" — was preserved and extended.

- **No Examples section** was added during initial assembly. The Skill Authoring Constraints require 2-3 I/O examples per section, but none of the winning variants provided runnable Elixir snippets — all were prose rules + mistake lists. This was called out as a quality gap in post-assembly review and addressed in a follow-up pass (see `fix(phoenix-liveview-composite): add examples, fix contradiction + detectors`, PR #20).

- **Composite body weighed in at ~165 lines of prose + lists** on first assembly, well under the 500-line Skill Authoring ceiling. The follow-up examples pass grew it to ~299 lines, still within budget.

---

### Open questions for future iterations

- **Score.py discrimination**: the L1 scorer didn't differentiate variants on 5 of 11 dimensions (both hit 1.000). Real fitness signal needs L2-L5 reviewers — L2 trigger accuracy, L3 trace-based behavioral analysis, L4 comparative pairwise ranking, L5 trait attribution. The seed run intentionally skipped these layers; the production pipeline should not.

- **Spawner diversity heuristic**: all 11 capability spawns were generated in parallel with minimal cross-dimension awareness. Some of the diversity angles overlapped (e.g., the mount-and-lifecycle `handle-params-first` option would have conflicted with the async-load winner differently than the other options). A future Spawner could read the other spawns in-flight and diversify explicitly.

- **Foundation-capability coupling**: the component-forward foundation demands `:id` on every LiveComponent and routes all DB work through contexts. Capabilities that assume a different stance (e.g., a LiveView-heavy foundation) would need different capability variants. The current single-foundation-per-family approach locks the capabilities to that stance — worth revisiting for meta-family composition.

- **Reconstruction authenticity**: the Engineer's integration report was never persisted during the original Step 1 run — it was only in the subagent's reply and lost to conversation compaction. This report is a post-facto reconstruction written by reading the 12 final winning variants directly. The conflicts are real and the resolutions in the composite match what a fresh reading would produce, but the exact phrasing and ordering differ from whatever the original Engineer wrote.

---

*This integration report was reconstructed post-facto from the 12 winning variants on 2026-04-11. The original Engineer's in-session integration report was lost during a context window compaction. The Step 1b workstream added a `scripts/mock_pipeline/persist_integration_report.py` helper so future seed runs will persist the Engineer's output at the time of assembly.*

frontmatter.description: Phoenix 1.7+ LiveView — component-forward architecture with verified routes, HEEx, streams, forms, async mount, pubsub, on_mount authz. Use when writing or reviewing Phoenix 1.7+ LiveView code. NOT for Phoenix 1.6 or older.
frontmatter.name: elixir-phoenix-liveview-composite

pareto_front.skill_md_content:
--- name: elixir-phoenix-liveview-composite description: Phoenix 1.7+ LiveView — component-forward architecture with verified routes, HEEx, streams, forms, async mount, pubsub, on_mount authz. Use when writing or reviewing Phoenix 1.7+ LiveView code. NOT for Phoenix 1.6 or older. --- # Phoenix 1.7+ LiveView — Composite Skill ## Quick Start The parent `Phoenix.LiveView` is a thin coordinator. Anything stateful lives in a `Phoenix.LiveComponent`. The parent owns URL state, session, and PubSub subscriptions; children own their own slice of state and talk upward via `send/2` or sideways via `send_update/2`. All rendering uses HEEx with the `~p` verified-route sigil, and all collections over ~100 items flow through `stream/3`. ## When to use - Writing a new Phoenix 1.7+ LiveView with more than one interactive region - Refactoring a bloated LiveView whose `handle_event` clauses exceed ~10 cases - Reviewing PRs where a single LiveView mutates many unrelated assigns - Converting a Phoenix 1.6 LiveView to 1.7+ idioms (verified routes, `<.link>`, streams, `assign_async`) - Introducing real-time updates, nested forms, or per-resource authorization to an existing LiveView module ## Architectural Skeleton (Foundation) Every LiveView begins as a thin parent. The parent's `mount/3` assigns only URL, session, and non-blocking state. `assign_async/3` calls are allowed (they run in a Task). Synchronous DB queries that block first paint belong in `handle_params/3`, not `mount/3`. For each distinct interactive region (a form, a list, a filter panel, a modal) create a `Phoenix.LiveComponent` under `lib/my_app_web/live/components/`. Give every LiveComponent a stable required `:id` assign and render it as `<.live_component module={...} id="..." ... />`. User events are handled inside the component's own `handle_event/3` and mutate only that component's socket. Child-to-parent signaling uses `send(self(), {:child_msg, payload})`, handled by the parent's `handle_info/2`; parent-to-child updates use `send_update/2` targeting a stable `id`. All database work is routed through context modules, called from the component that owns the data — never from the parent. Forms are always built with `to_form/2` inside the component that owns them, never lifted to the parent. The parent never reaches into a child's socket with `assign/3`. ## HEEx + Verified Routes Use the `~p` sigil for every route. `~p"/users/#{id}"` replaces `Routes.user_path(@socket, :show, id)`. Use HEEx directives `:if={...}` and `:for={x <- @xs}` instead of the legacy `<%= if %>` and `<%= for %>` Eex blocks. Prefer `<.link navigate={~p"/users"}>` over the removed `live_link` / `live_redirect` helpers, and `<.link patch={~p"/users?sort=name"}>` for same-LiveView URL updates. Template bodies always use the `~H` sigil. ### Example def render(assigns) do ~H""" <.link navigate={~p"/users/new"}>New user
  • <.link patch={~p"/users?sort=#{user.id}"}> <%= user.name %>
""" end Common mistakes: 1. Mixing old `Routes.*_path` helpers with new `~p` sigils in the same module. 2. Leaving `<%= for item <- @items do %>` blocks that should be `:for={item <- @items}` directives. 3. Using `` where `<.link navigate={~p"/x"}>` is required for client-side routing. ## Function Components + Slots Function components are rendering primitives, not logic holders. Declare every input with `attr :name, :type, required: true` — no untyped assigns. Declare every child region with `slot :inner_block` or a named `slot :actions`. The body is HEEx + `render_slot/1` + `~H` interpolation only. No `case`, no branching on business rules, no Ecto calls, no `Repo.get/2`. If a rendering decision is needed — a badge color for a status, a class for an alert variant — compute it in the parent LiveView via `assign/3` and pass the resolved value in as an attr. Co-locate these components under `MyAppWeb.CoreComponents` or a feature-scoped `*Components` module. Unit-test them with `Phoenix.LiveViewTest.render_component/2` passing literal attrs. ### Example attr :label, :string, required: true attr :badge_class, :string, required: true slot :actions def status_card(assigns) do ~H"""
<%= @label %>
<%= render_slot(@actions) %>
""" end Common mistakes: 1. Calling `Repo.get/2` or context functions inside a function component. 2. Using `case @status do ... end` for class selection — precompute in the parent. 3. Accepting a raw `%Ecto.Changeset{}` and branching on `changeset.valid?`. 4. Adding `@socket` or `assign/3` calls — function components have no socket. 5. Omitting `attr` declarations "just for this one prop". ## LiveComponents (Stateful) Treat `update/2` as the single choke point where parent-supplied props become socket assigns. It is the ONLY function that calls `assign(socket, :prop, assigns.prop)` for external data. Any dependent local state (filtered lists, derived labels) is also computed inside `update/2`, in the same pass. `handle_event/3` mutates only component-owned UI state like `:open?` or `:selected_id`; it must never re-read props from `assigns`. When the parent needs to push a mid-lifecycle change, it calls `send_update(MyComponent, id: "x", prop: new_value)`. For child-to-parent signaling, use `send(self(), {:child_event, @myself, payload})` and route it through the parent's `handle_info/2`. The parent's `<.live_component />` call MUST include `:id`. ### Example defmodule MyAppWeb.FilterPanel do use MyAppWeb, :live_component def update(%{users: users, query: q} = assigns, socket) do {:ok, socket |> assign(assigns) |> assign(:visible, Enum.filter(users, &String.contains?(&1.name, q)))} end def handle_event("toggle", _, socket) do {:noreply, assign(socket, :open?, not socket.assigns.open?)} end end Common mistakes: 1. Calling `assign(socket, :prop, assigns.prop)` inside `handle_event/3`. Props belong in `update/2` only. 2. Forgetting `:id` on `<.live_component />`. Stateful components REQUIRE a stable id. 3. Deriving filtered or sorted data in the template instead of in `update/2`. 4. Using `send_update/3` with partial assigns and expecting old props to persist. 5. Trying to mutate parent state by reaching "up" through assigns. ## Forms + Nested Associations Nested forms use `cast_assoc` plus `<.inputs_for>`. In the schema, declare `has_many :children, Child, on_replace: :delete`. In the changeset, call `cast_assoc(:children, with: &Child.changeset/2, sort_param: :children_sort, drop_param: :children_drop)`. In the LiveComponent that owns the parent form, assign the form once with `to_form(Parents.change_parent(parent))`. Render children with `<.inputs_for :let={cf} field={@form[:children]}>`. Add a child with a button posting `parent[children_sort][]=new`; remove a child with `parent[children_drop][]=` plus a hidden input inside the row. On the `"validate"` event, rebuild via `to_form(changeset, action: :validate)`. On `"save"`, call the context's `update_parent/2`; on `{:error, changeset}`, re-assign `to_form(changeset)`. ### Example def update(%{parent: parent} = assigns, socket) do form = Parents.change_parent(parent) |> to_form() {:ok, socket |> assign(assigns) |> assign(:form, form)} end def handle_event("validate", %{"parent" => params}, socket) do changeset = socket.assigns.parent |> Parents.change_parent(params) |> Map.put(:action, :validate) {:noreply, assign(socket, :form, to_form(changeset))} end Common mistakes: 1. Forgetting `on_replace: :delete` on the `has_many` — removed rows reappear after save. 2. Using `<.inputs_for>` without a matching hidden `sort_param` input — row order scrambles. 3. Rebuilding child rows manually in `handle_event` instead of letting `cast_assoc` + `:drop_param` do it. 4. Putting the nested form in the parent LiveView instead of the owning LiveComponent. 5. Calling `to_form/2` on the child changeset directly. ## Streams + Collections Use `Phoenix.LiveView.stream/3` for any collection over ~100 items or any list that grows in response to realtime updates. `stream(socket, :messages, initial_list)` replaces `assign(socket, :messages, list)`. In the template, iterate with `
...
` — the `id` destructuring and element id are both required. Append with `stream_insert(socket, :messages, new_msg)`, remove with `stream_delete(socket, :messages, msg)`, replace with `stream_insert(socket, :messages, updated_msg)` using the same id. Streams never land in regular `@assigns`; they live under `@streams`. ### Example def mount(_params, _session, socket) do {:ok, stream(socket, :messages, Chat.list_messages())} end def handle_info({:new_message, msg}, socket) do {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :messages, msg, at: 0)} end def render(assigns) do ~H"""
<%= msg.body %>
""" end Common mistakes: 1. Holding a large list in `@messages` and re-rendering on every insert. 2. Forgetting the `id={id}` attribute on the iterated DOM node. 3. Calling `Enum.count/1` on `@streams.messages` — streams are opaque; track length in a separate assign. ## Mount + Lifecycle (Async Load) Use `assign_async/3` (Phoenix 1.7+) for initial data loads that touch the database. In `mount/3`, return `{:ok, assign_async(socket, :users, fn -> {:ok, %{users: Accounts.list_users()}} end)}`. The template branches on `@users.loading`, `@users.ok?`, and `@users.result` — render a skeleton while loading, render the list when `ok?`, and render an error state via `@users.failed` when the async fun returns `{:error, reason}`. In `handle_params/3`, re-run `assign_async` whenever URL params change. For multiple independent loads, call `assign_async` once per key so each resolves on its own timeline. Do not close over the whole `socket` inside the async function — capture only the primitive ids and params you need. ### Example defmodule MyAppWeb.UsersLive do use MyAppWeb, :live_view def mount(_params, _session, socket) do {:ok, socket |> assign_async(:users, fn -> {:ok, %{users: MyApp.Accounts.list_users()}} end) |> assign_async(:stats, fn -> {:ok, %{stats: MyApp.Accounts.summary()}} end)} end def render(assigns) do ~H"""
Loading...
<.user_list :if={@users.ok?} users={@users.result} />
Failed to load users
""" end end Common mistakes: 1. Synchronously calling `Repo.all/1` directly in mount, outside the async fun — blocks first paint. 2. Forgetting to handle `@users.loading` in the template. 3. Using `assign_async` for cheap in-memory assigns where the ceremony outweighs the benefit. 4. Closing over the full `socket` inside the async fun — the closure leaks the entire assigns map. ## Event Handlers + handle_info Every `handle_event/3` clause is three lines: parse the incoming params into an `%Action{type: _, payload: _}` struct, call `handle_action(action, socket)`, and return its result directly. All state-change logic lives in `handle_action/2`, a private pure function that pattern-matches on `action.type` and returns `{:noreply, socket}`. `handle_info/2` clauses that mutate state do the same dance: convert the message into an `%Action{}` first, then delegate. Keep `handle_action/2` under ~10 clauses per component — beyond that, split the component. The payoff: `handle_action/2` is unit-testable with plain ExUnit against a built socket, with no `LiveViewTest` harness required. ### Example def handle_event("save", %{"user" => params}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :save, payload: %{attrs: params}}, socket) end def handle_info({:user_updated, user}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :refresh, payload: %{user: user}}, socket) end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :save, payload: %{attrs: attrs}}, socket) do case MyApp.Accounts.create_user(attrs) do {:ok, user} -> {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :users, user)} {:error, cs} -> {:noreply, assign(socket, :form, to_form(cs))} end end Common mistakes: 1. Putting `Repo.insert` or `Ecto.Changeset` logic inline in `handle_event`. 2. Letting `%Action{}` carry raw `params` maps — coerce to typed fields up front. 3. Skipping the funnel for "simple" events — inconsistency destroys the testability guarantee. 4. Forgetting to route `handle_info` state changes through `handle_action/2`. ## PubSub + Realtime The Ecto context is the single source of broadcast events. Schema writes and `Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast` live side-by-side in one function, so every caller — LiveView, Oban job, controller — fans out identically. In `MyApp.Chat`, define a private `broadcast/2` helper that receives `{:ok, result}` tuples and fans out named events. Pipe every write through it: `create_message/1`, `update_message/2`, `delete_message/1`. In the parent LiveView's `mount/3`, subscribe only when `connected?(socket)` is true: `if connected?(socket), do: Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{room_id}")`. Handle incoming events in the parent and route them into children via `send_update/2` when a LiveComponent owns that slice. Never broadcast before `Repo.insert` has succeeded — always pipe the `{:ok, _}` tuple through the helper, and always include an `{:error, _}` clause to short-circuit the broadcast. ### Example defmodule MyApp.Chat do def create_message(attrs) do %Message{} |> Message.changeset(attrs) |> Repo.insert() |> broadcast(:message_created) end defp broadcast({:ok, %Message{room_id: id} = msg}, event) do Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{id}", {event, msg}) {:ok, msg} end defp broadcast({:error, _} = err, _event), do: err end Common mistakes: 1. Calling `Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast` directly from `handle_event/3` — bypasses the single source. 2. Broadcasting before `Repo.insert` returns `{:ok, _}`. 3. Making `broadcast/2` public instead of `defp` — invites ad-hoc callers. 4. Subscribing without the `connected?(socket)` guard — subscribes twice and leaks processes. ## Navigation Patterns Cross-LiveView navigation uses `push_navigate(socket, to: ~p"/...")` — mounts a fresh LiveView process. Same-LiveView URL updates (a sort change, a filter applied, a tab swap) use `push_patch(socket, to: ~p"/...")` and are handled in `handle_params/3`. Templates always use `<.link navigate={~p"/users/#{user}"}>...` for forward navigation and `<.link patch={~p"/users?sort=name"}>` for in-place patches. The legacy `live_link`, `live_patch`, and `live_redirect` helpers were removed in 1.7 and must never appear in new code. ### Example def handle_event("sort", %{"by" => field}, socket) do {:noreply, push_patch(socket, to: ~p"/users?sort=#{field}")} end def handle_event("view", %{"id" => id}, socket) do {:noreply, push_navigate(socket, to: ~p"/users/#{id}")} end def handle_params(%{"sort" => field}, _uri, socket) do {:noreply, assign(socket, :sort_by, field)} end Common mistakes: 1. Using `push_redirect` (deprecated) instead of `push_navigate`. 2. Patching when the target is a different LiveView — only `push_navigate` remounts. 3. Hardcoding URL strings instead of using `~p`. ## Auth + Authz A single `on_mount` hook authenticates the user AND authorizes access to the specific resource identified by `params["id"]`. If either check fails, the hook returns `{:halt, socket |> put_flash(...) |> redirect(...)}` before the LiveView ever mounts. Define the hook in `MyAppWeb.UserAuth` — it authenticates AND fetches the resource scoped to the user via the context, e.g. `Posts.fetch_for_user/2` which returns `{:ok, post}` only when the user owns the post (or is an admin). Wire the hook per-route in the router: `live_session :post_scoped, on_mount: [{MyAppWeb.UserAuth, :scope_post}]`. Inside `mount/3`, trust `socket.assigns.post` — no re-fetch, no re-check. Pass it to LiveComponents as a prop. `handle_event/3` clauses operate on `socket.assigns.post` directly; no `if current_user.id == post.user_id` checks. Mutations still take `user` as a defense-in-depth arg at the context layer: `Posts.update_post(post, attrs, user)`. ### Example defmodule MyAppWeb.UserAuth do import Phoenix.LiveView import Phoenix.Component def on_mount(:scope_post, %{"id" => id}, _session, socket) do user = socket.assigns.current_user case MyApp.Posts.fetch_for_user(id, user) do {:ok, post} -> {:cont, assign(socket, :post, post)} {:error, _} -> {:halt, socket |> put_flash(:error, "Not found") |> redirect(to: ~p"/")} end end end Common mistakes: 1. Re-fetching the post in `mount/3` after the hook already assigned it. 2. Putting the scoped hook on a `live_session` that mixes scoped and unscoped routes. 3. Forgetting the `params["id"]` missing case in the hook. 4. Returning `{:cont, socket}` on authz failure instead of `{:halt, ...}`. 5. Letting LiveComponents re-fetch the resource by id from their own assigns. ## Anti-Patterns Catalog Every anti-pattern below ships with a mechanical detector — grep, AST probe, or Credo check — so reviewers can find violations in any codebase by running the detector top-to-bottom. 1. **DB queries in mount/3** — `mount` runs twice (HTTP + WebSocket), duplicating every query. Detector: `rg -n 'def mount' -A 20 -g 'lib/**/*_live.ex' | rg -B1 'Repo\.(all|one|get|preload)'`. Fix: wrap in `assign_async` or guard with `if connected?(socket), do: load(), else: assign(socket, :items, [])`. 2. **PubSub.subscribe without connected?** — subscribes twice on first load and leaks processes on reconnect. Detector: `rg -nP 'Phoenix\.PubSub\.subscribe' lib/ | rg -v 'connected\?'`. Credo: custom `Credo.Check.Warning.UnguardedPubSubSubscribe`. 3. **Routes.*_path / Routes.*_url leftovers** — pre-1.7 helpers; verified routes are mandatory. Detector: `rg -n 'Routes\.\w+_(path|url)' lib/ test/`. Rewrite as `~p"/users/#{id}"`. 4. **Bare %Changeset{} passed to <.form>** — skips `to_form/2`, breaks nested association handling. Detector: `rg -nP '<\.form [^>]*for=\{@(?!.*to_form)' lib/`. 5. **Enum over assigns for lists >100 items** — should be `stream/3`. Heuristic: context function name starts with `list_` and the HEEx uses `:for={item <- @items}` where `@items` is assigned via `assign`, not `stream`. 6. **assign_new for values needing fresh compute** — stale after reconnect because `assign_new` only runs when the key is missing. Detector: `rg -nP 'assign_new.*fn ->' lib/ -A 3 | rg -B1 '(Repo|utc_now)'`. 7. **Legacy navigation helpers** — `live_link`, `live_patch`, `live_redirect` removed in 1.7. Detector: `rg -nP '\b(live_link|live_patch|live_redirect)\b' lib/`. Rewrite to `<.link navigate={~p"..."}>` or `<.link patch={~p"..."}>`. 8. **LiveComponent without :id** — silently becomes stateless, loses assigns between renders. Detector: `rg -n '<\.live_component' lib/ | rg -v 'id='`. 9. **Repo calls inside function components** — function components are pure rendering; queries belong in the parent LiveView or LiveComponent. Detector: `rg -n 'def \w+\(assigns\)' -g 'lib/**/components*.ex' -A 20 | rg 'Repo\.'`. 10. **PubSub.broadcast called from handle_event** — broadcasts should be context-owned so every caller fans out identically. Detector: `rg -n 'def handle_event' -g 'lib/**/*_live.ex' -A 15 | rg 'PubSub\.broadcast'`. 11. **Props assigned outside update/2** — violates the LiveComponent update-discipline contract. Detector: `rg -nP 'def handle_event.*\n.*assign\(socket, :\w+, assigns\.' lib/` (multiline). 12. **Raw params map stuffed into %Action{}** — defeats the event-to-action funnel's testability. Detector: AST probe for `%Action{payload: params}` where `params` is the raw event params binding. 13. **Re-fetching a resource after on_mount scoped it** — wastes a query and opens a TOCTOU gap. Detector: `rg -n 'on_mount.*:scope_' -g 'lib/**/*_live.ex' -A 30 | rg 'Posts\.get_post\|Repo\.get'`. 14. **Template <%= for %> instead of :for=** — legacy Eex inside HEEx; bypasses optimization. Detector: `rg -nP '<%=\s*for\s+' -g 'lib/**/*.html.heex' -g 'lib/**/*_live.ex'`.

supporting_files.assets/migration_checklist.md:
# Phoenix 1.6 -> 1.7+ LiveView Migration Checklist Upgrade a Phoenix 1.6 LiveView codebase to 1.7+ idioms: verified routes (`~p`), the unified `<.link>` component, HEEx directives (`:for`/`:if`), `to_form/2` forms, `stream/3` collections, `assign_async/3` mounts, typed function components, stateful `LiveComponent` discipline, `on_mount` auth hooks, and updated tests. Each step gives you: (1) a **grep command** to find occurrences, (2) a **1.6 -> 1.7 diff**, and (3) a **gotcha** to watch for. Work the steps in order — later steps assume earlier ones have landed. --- ## Before you start 1. **Version check.** In `mix.exs` confirm `{:phoenix, "~> 1.7.0"}` and `{:phoenix_live_view, "~> 0.20.0"}` (or newer). Run `mix deps.get` and `mix compile` on a clean baseline. 2. **Back up.** Create a branch (`git checkout -b liveview-1.7-migration`) or tag (`git tag pre-liveview-1.7`). Do NOT run codemods against uncommitted work. 3. **Feature-flag strategy.** Migrate one LiveView at a time. Use `live_session` blocks in the router to isolate migrated routes from un-migrated ones. 4. **Run the tests.** `mix test` must pass on the baseline — otherwise you can't tell what the migration broke vs. what was already broken. --- ## Step 1: Routes — `Routes.*_path` -> `~p` Verified routes are checked at compile time. ```bash rg -n 'Routes\.\w+_(path|url)' lib/ test/ ``` ```diff - <%= link "New user", to: Routes.user_path(@socket, :new) %> + <.link navigate={~p"/users/new"}>New user - redirect(to: Routes.user_path(socket, :show, user.id)) + push_navigate(socket, to: ~p"/users/#{user.id}") ``` **Gotcha:** `~p"/users/#{user}"` requires `User` to implement `Phoenix.Param` (usually via `@derive Phoenix.Param` or a `@primary_key` field). Raw UUID strings always work. --- ## Step 2: Templates — `<%= for %>` / `<%= if %>` / `~L` -> HEEx ```bash rg -nP '<%=\s*(for|if)\s+' lib/ -g '*.heex' -g '*.ex' -g '*.eex' rg -nP 'sigil_L|~L"""' lib/ ``` ```diff - <%= for user <- @users do %> - <%= if user.active do %> -
  • <%= user.name %>
    • - <% end %> - <% end %> +
  • + <%= user.name %> +
    • - ~L""" + ~H""" ``` **Gotcha:** When `:for` and `:if` sit on the same element, `:for` runs first and `:if` filters each iteration. If you need "hide the whole block," wrap it in a parent element that carries `:if` — don't rely on `:if` inside a loopless block that must disappear entirely. --- ## Step 3: Navigation — legacy helpers -> `<.link>` / `push_navigate` `live_link`, `live_patch`, `live_redirect`, `push_redirect` all removed. ```bash rg -nP '\b(live_link|live_patch|live_redirect|push_redirect)\b' lib/ ``` ```diff - <%= live_patch "Sort", to: "/users?sort=name" %> + <.link patch={~p"/users?sort=name"}>Sort - <%= live_redirect "Profile", to: Routes.profile_path(@socket, :show) %> + <.link navigate={~p"/profile"}>Profile - {:noreply, push_redirect(socket, to: "/dashboard")} + {:noreply, push_navigate(socket, to: ~p"/dashboard")} ``` **Gotcha:** `patch` is same-LiveView-module-only — crossing LiveView boundaries with `<.link patch={...}>` crashes. Use `patch` for sort/filter/ tab URL updates; `navigate` to show a different resource. --- ## Step 4: Forms — bare changesets -> `to_form/2` ```bash rg -nP '<\.form [^>]*for=\{@(?!.*to_form)' lib/ rg -n 'form_for\s*\(' lib/ -g '*.heex' -g '*.ex' ``` ```diff - {:ok, assign(socket, :changeset, Accounts.change_user(%User{}))} + form = Accounts.change_user(%User{}) |> to_form() + {:ok, assign(socket, :form, form)} - <%= form_for @changeset, "#", [phx_change: "validate"], fn f -> %> - <%= label f, :name %> - <%= text_input f, :name %> - <%= error_tag f, :name %> - <% end %> + <.form for={@form} phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save"> + <.input field={@form[:name]} type="text" label="Name" /> + ``` **Gotcha:** On `"validate"`, rebuild via `changeset |> Map.put(:action, :validate) |> to_form()`. Without `:action`, `<.input>` won't render errors because the changeset looks fresh. Nested `cast_assoc` also needs `on_replace: :delete` on the parent `has_many` or removed rows reappear after save. --- ## Step 5: Collections — assigns -> `stream/3` Any list over ~100 items, or any realtime-updated list, should become a stream. ```bash rg -nP 'phx-update="(append|prepend)"' lib/ ``` ```diff - {:ok, assign(socket, :messages, Chat.list_messages())} + {:ok, stream(socket, :messages, Chat.list_messages())} - def handle_info({:new, msg}, socket) do - {:noreply, assign(socket, :messages, [msg | socket.assigns.messages])} - end + def handle_info({:new, msg}, socket) do + {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :messages, msg, at: 0)} + end -
    -
    <%= msg.body %>
    -
    +
    +
    + <%= msg.body %> +
    +
    ``` **Gotcha:** Streams are opaque — no `Enum.count/1`, no `Enum.filter/1`. If the template shows "N items", assign the length as a separate regular assign and update it alongside every `stream_insert` / `stream_delete`. Every iterated DOM node MUST carry `id={dom_id}` or the DOM patcher crashes. --- ## Step 6: Mount — DB queries -> `handle_params/3` or `assign_async/3` `mount/3` runs twice (HTTP + WebSocket), so DB calls there double every query and block first paint. ```bash rg -nP 'def mount\(' lib/ -A 15 | rg -B 1 'Repo\.(all|one|get|preload)' ``` ```diff - def mount(_params, _session, socket) do - users = Accounts.list_users() - {:ok, assign(socket, :users, users)} - end + def mount(_params, _session, socket) do + {:ok, stream(socket, :users, [])} + end + + def handle_params(params, _uri, socket) do + users = Accounts.list_users(sort: params["sort"] || "name") + {:noreply, stream(socket, :users, users, reset: true)} + end ``` For slow queries that shouldn't block first paint, use `assign_async/3`: ```diff + def mount(_params, _session, socket) do + {:ok, + assign_async(socket, :users, fn -> + {:ok, %{users: Accounts.list_users()}} + end)} + end ``` **Gotcha:** Inside `assign_async` funs, never close over the whole `socket` — the closure leaks the entire assigns map into the Task process. Capture only the primitive ids and params you need (bind `user_id = ...` outside the fun, then reference `user_id` inside). --- ## Step 7: Render helpers -> function components with `attr` ```bash rg -nP 'def \w+\(assigns\)\s*do' lib/ -g '*_view.ex' -g '*_html.ex' ``` ```diff - def badge(assigns) do - ~H""" - <%= @label %> - """ - end + attr :label, :string, required: true + attr :variant, :string, default: "default", values: ~w(default success warning error) + slot :inner_block + + def badge(assigns) do + ~H""" + + <%= @label %> + <%= render_slot(@inner_block) %> + + """ + end ``` **Gotcha:** Function components are **pure rendering** — no `Repo.*` calls, no `case` on business rules, no changeset inspection. Precompute conditional classes in the caller and pass them as attrs. Keeping components dumb is what makes them testable via `render_component/2`. --- ## Step 8: LiveComponents — require `:id`, move state to `update/2` Stateful `LiveComponent` calls MUST carry `:id` — without it they silently degrade to stateless and lose assigns between renders. ```bash rg -nP '<\.live_component\b' lib/ | rg -v 'id=' ``` ```diff - <%= live_component @socket, MyAppWeb.FilterPanel, users: @users, query: @q %> + <.live_component + module={MyAppWeb.FilterPanel} + id="users-filter-panel" + users={@users} + query={@q} + /> - def handle_event("apply", %{"q" => q}, socket) do - users = Accounts.search(socket.assigns.users, q) - {:noreply, assign(socket, :users, users)} - end + def update(%{users: users, query: q} = assigns, socket) do + {:ok, + socket + |> assign(assigns) + |> assign(:visible, Enum.filter(users, &String.contains?(&1.name, q)))} + end + + def handle_event("toggle", _, socket) do + {:noreply, assign(socket, :open?, not socket.assigns.open?)} + end ``` **Gotcha:** `handle_event/3` inside a LiveComponent MUST NOT call `assign(socket, :some_prop, assigns.some_prop)` — props only enter through `update/2`. Break that rule and `send_update/3` from the parent will silently overwrite your handler's work on the next render. --- ## Step 9: Auth — pipeline auth -> `on_mount` hooks + `live_session` Controller-pipeline auth doesn't run inside the LiveView process, so a LiveView can mount even after HTTP rejection. `on_mount` hooks run every mount (HTTP + WebSocket) and are the only safe place to enforce LiveView auth. ```bash rg -nP 'live\s+"[^"]+",\s+\w+Live' lib/ | rg -v 'live_session' rg -n 'plug :require_authenticated_user' lib/ ``` ```diff - pipeline :authenticated do - plug :require_authenticated_user - end - scope "/", MyAppWeb do - pipe_through [:browser, :authenticated] - live "/posts/:id", PostLive.Show - end + scope "/", MyAppWeb do + pipe_through :browser + live_session :post_scoped, + on_mount: [{MyAppWeb.UserAuth, :scope_post}] do + live "/posts/:id", PostLive.Show + end + end + def on_mount(:scope_post, %{"id" => id}, _session, socket) do + user = socket.assigns.current_user + case MyApp.Posts.fetch_for_user(id, user) do + {:ok, post} -> {:cont, assign(socket, :post, post)} + {:error, _} -> {:halt, socket |> put_flash(:error, "Not found") |> redirect(to: ~p"/")} + end + end ``` **Gotcha:** `on_mount` hooks pattern-match on `unsigned_params`. If the route has `:id` but your hook pattern is `%{}`, Elixir still matches and runs silently for requests missing `:id`. Always pattern-match explicitly on the keys you need and add a fallback clause that halts. --- ## Step 10: Tests — `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` updates ```bash rg -nP 'live_isolated|Phoenix\.ConnTest\.dispatch' test/ ``` ```diff - {:ok, view, html} = live_isolated(conn, MyAppWeb.UsersLive, session: %{}) + {:ok, view, html} = live(conn, ~p"/users") + view |> element("a", "Email") |> render_click() + assert_patched(view, ~p"/users?sort=email") + html = render_component(&MyAppWeb.Components.badge/1, label: "ok", variant: "success") + assert html =~ "badge-success" ``` **Gotcha:** `render(view)` won't show post-mount stream inserts — streams render DOM-only on the client after mount, so server-side HTML won't contain them. Use `render(view)` for first-paint assertions, `has_element?/3` for post-event state, and `render_async(view)` when waiting for a broadcast to land. --- ## Verification Run after every step, and absolutely before merging: 1. **Compile clean.** `mix compile --warnings-as-errors` 2. **Run the package validator.** `bash "${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/scripts/validate.sh"` 3. **Run tests twice** — once normally, once with `mix test --seed 0` to catch order-dependent flakes. 4. **Grep for legacy patterns one more time** (copy-paste from old branches sneaks back): ```bash rg -nP '\b(live_link|live_patch|live_redirect|push_redirect)\b' lib/ rg -n 'Routes\.\w+_(path|url)' lib/ rg -nP '<%=\s*(for|if)\s+' lib/ -g '*.heex' -g '*.ex' ``` 5. **Click through every migrated route in a browser** — verified routes with dynamic params only surface at runtime. If all five pass, ship. If any fail, fix and re-run before merging — partial migration is the worst of both worlds because 1.6 and 1.7 patterns compile side-by-side but behave subtly differently under load.

defmodule MyAppWeb.UserFormComponent do @moduledoc """ Starter template for a Phoenix 1.7+ `Phoenix.LiveComponent`. ## update/2 discipline `update/2` is the single choke point where parent-supplied props become socket assigns. It is the ONLY function that calls `assign(socket, :prop, assigns.prop)` for external data — and it also computes any dependent local state (a form, a filtered list, a derived label) in the same pass. `handle_event/3` mutates only component-owned UI state like `:open?`, `:selected_id`, or a form changeset under `:form`. It NEVER re-reads props from `assigns` — props can only enter via `update/2`. ## Parent/child signalling - Parent -> child: the parent calls `send_update(MyAppWeb.UserFormComponent, id: "user-form-42", user: user)`. `send_update/3` triggers another `update/2` invocation, merging the new assigns into the existing socket (old props persist unless overwritten). - Child -> parent: the component calls `send(self(), {:component_event, @myself, {:user_saved, user}})` and the parent LiveView's `handle_info/2` funnels the tuple through its own `handle_action/2`. Copy this file into `lib/my_app_web/live/components/`, rename the module, and replace `MyApp.Accounts` / `:user` / `User` with your own context and resource. """ use Phoenix.LiveComponent # TODO: replace with your own context module and schema alias MyApp.Accounts alias MyApp.Accounts.User # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # update/2 — the ONLY place props become assigns. Derived state (the form) # is computed here in the same pass so `handle_event/3` never re-reads props. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- @impl true def update(%{user: %User{} = user} = assigns, socket) do changeset = Accounts.change_user(user) socket = socket |> assign(assigns) |> assign_new(:open?, fn -> true end) |> assign(:form, to_form(changeset)) {:ok, socket} end # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # handle_event/3 — targeted via `phx-target={@myself}` so events route to # this component rather than the parent LiveView. Only component-owned state # is mutated here. No `Repo.*` calls, no context fetches beyond the final # save (which is a user-driven write, not a load). # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- @impl true def handle_event("validate", %{"user" => params}, socket) do changeset = socket.assigns.user |> Accounts.change_user(params) |> Map.put(:action, :validate) {:noreply, assign(socket, :form, to_form(changeset))} end def handle_event("save", %{"user" => params}, socket) do save_user(socket, socket.assigns.action, params) end def handle_event("toggle", _payload, socket) do {:noreply, assign(socket, :open?, not socket.assigns.open?)} end def handle_event("cancel", _payload, socket) do # Purely component-owned state change — no DB calls. send(self(), {:component_event, socket.assigns.myself, :cancelled}) {:noreply, assign(socket, :open?, false)} end # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Private helpers — business-logic funnel and parent notification. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- defp save_user(socket, :new, params) do case Accounts.create_user(params) do {:ok, user} -> notify_parent(socket, {:user_saved, user}) {:noreply, socket |> put_flash(:info, "User created") |> assign(:form, to_form(Accounts.change_user(%User{})))} {:error, %Ecto.Changeset{} = changeset} -> {:noreply, assign(socket, :form, to_form(changeset))} end end defp save_user(socket, :edit, params) do case Accounts.update_user(socket.assigns.user, params) do {:ok, user} -> notify_parent(socket, {:user_saved, user}) {:noreply, socket |> put_flash(:info, "User updated") |> assign(:form, to_form(Accounts.change_user(user)))} {:error, %Ecto.Changeset{} = changeset} -> {:noreply, assign(socket, :form, to_form(changeset))} end end # Child-to-parent pattern. The parent LiveView's `handle_info/2` matches # on `{:component_event, _from, payload}` and routes through its # `handle_action/2`. `@myself` identifies which component instance fired. defp notify_parent(socket, payload) do send(self(), {:component_event, socket.assigns.myself, payload}) end # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Render — component-owned <.form>. The form lives here, never lifted to # the parent. Every phx-* attribute is `phx-target={@myself}`. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- @impl true def render(assigns) do ~H"""

<%= if @action == :new, do: "New user", else: "Edit user" %>

<.form :if={@open?} for={@form} id={"#{@id}-form"} phx-target={@myself} phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save" > <.input field={@form[:name]} type="text" label="Name" required /> <.input field={@form[:email]} type="email" label="Email" required /> <.input field={@form[:role]} type="select" label="Role" options={~w(admin member guest)} />
""" end # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Parent-side send_update/3 examples (kept here as reference — NOT called # from within this component). Place these in the parent LiveView. # # To push a new user prop into this component mid-lifecycle: # # Phoenix.LiveView.send_update( # MyAppWeb.UserFormComponent, # id: "user-form-42", # user: refreshed_user # ) # # To switch the component's action (e.g. after a successful save): # # Phoenix.LiveView.send_update( # MyAppWeb.UserFormComponent, # id: "user-form-42", # action: :edit, # user: saved_user # ) # # Remember: `send_update/3` merges assigns — keys you don't pass retain # their previous values. Always pass every key that depends on the new # data so derived state in `update/2` recomputes correctly. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- end
