defmodule MyAppWeb.ResourceLive.Index do @moduledoc """ Starter template for a Phoenix 1.7+ LiveView following the component-forward stance: the parent LiveView is a thin coordinator that owns URL state, session, and PubSub subscriptions. All interactive regions (forms, lists, filter panels, modals) are delegated to `Phoenix.LiveComponent` children. Database queries never run from this module's `mount/3`; they flow through the context module (`MyApp.Accounts` below) inside `handle_params/3` or via `assign_async/3`. Every `handle_event/3` and `handle_info/2` clause funnels through `handle_action/2`, so business logic stays unit-testable against a built socket without `Phoenix.LiveViewTest`. Copy this file into `lib/my_app_web/live/`, rename the module, search for every `TODO:` marker, and replace `MyApp.Accounts` / `:users` / `User` with your own context and resource. """ use MyAppWeb, :live_view # TODO: replace with your own context module and resource alias MyApp.Accounts alias MyApp.Accounts.User # TODO: point at your own LiveComponents alias MyAppWeb.UserFormComponent alias MyAppWeb.FilterPanelComponent # Typed envelope for the event-to-action funnel. Keeps `handle_action/2` # pattern matches narrow and raw params out of business logic. defmodule Action do @moduledoc false defstruct [:type, :payload] end # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Mount — URL/session state only, NO blocking DB queries. Streams are # initialised here and populated in `handle_params/3`. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- @impl true def mount(_params, session, socket) do if connected?(socket) do # TODO: scope the topic to the current tenant / room / user Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(MyApp.PubSub, "users:lobby") end socket = socket |> assign(:page_title, "Users") |> assign(:current_user_id, session["current_user_id"]) |> assign(:sort_by, "name") |> assign(:query, "") |> assign(:selected_user, nil) # Streams are initialised in `mount/3` but populated in `handle_params/3`. |> stream(:users, []) # Async load for a non-critical summary card that shouldn't block paint. |> assign_async(:stats, fn -> # TODO: replace with your own summary context call {:ok, %{stats: Accounts.summary()}} end) {:ok, socket} end # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # handle_params — reads URL state, validates, loads data through the context, # and populates streams. Re-runs whenever live_patch updates the URL. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- @impl true def handle_params(params, _uri, socket) do sort_by = validate_sort(Map.get(params, "sort", "name")) query = Map.get(params, "q", "") |> String.slice(0, 200) # TODO: replace with your own context call. Keep this synchronous for # small result sets; swap for `assign_async/3` when the query is slow. users = Accounts.list_users(sort: sort_by, query: query) selected_user = case params do %{"id" => id} -> case Accounts.fetch_user(id) do {:ok, user} -> user {:error, _} -> nil end _ -> nil end socket = socket |> assign(:sort_by, sort_by) |> assign(:query, query) |> assign(:selected_user, selected_user) |> stream(:users, users, reset: true) {:noreply, socket} end defp validate_sort(field) when field in ~w(name email inserted_at), do: field defp validate_sort(_), do: "name" # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Event funnel — every handle_event/3 clause converts the incoming params # into a typed %Action{} and delegates to handle_action/2. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- @impl true def handle_event("sort", %{"by" => field}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :sort, payload: %{field: field}}, socket) end def handle_event("search", %{"q" => q}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :search, payload: %{query: q}}, socket) end def handle_event("select", %{"id" => id}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :select, payload: %{id: id}}, socket) end def handle_event("delete", %{"id" => id}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :delete, payload: %{id: id}}, socket) end # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # PubSub / in-process messages — same funnel pattern. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- @impl true def handle_info({:user_created, %User{} = user}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :refresh_insert, payload: %{user: user}}, socket) end def handle_info({:user_updated, %User{} = user}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :refresh_update, payload: %{user: user}}, socket) end def handle_info({:user_deleted, %User{} = user}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :refresh_delete, payload: %{user: user}}, socket) end # Child-to-parent signal from a LiveComponent via # `send(self(), {:component_event, @myself, payload})`. def handle_info({:component_event, _from, {:user_saved, user}}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :refresh_update, payload: %{user: user}}, socket) end # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # handle_action/2 — pure dispatcher. Unit-testable with plain ExUnit against # a built socket. Keep under ~10 clauses per LiveView; split the view when # this grows past that. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- defp handle_action(%Action{type: :sort, payload: %{field: field}}, socket) do {:noreply, push_patch(socket, to: ~p"/users?sort=#{field}")} end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :search, payload: %{query: q}}, socket) do {:noreply, push_patch(socket, to: ~p"/users?q=#{q}")} end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :select, payload: %{id: id}}, socket) do {:noreply, push_navigate(socket, to: ~p"/users/#{id}")} end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :delete, payload: %{id: id}}, socket) do # TODO: swap for your authorized delete path and error handling case Accounts.fetch_user(id) do {:ok, user} -> case Accounts.delete_user(user) do {:ok, _} -> {:noreply, socket |> stream_delete(:users, user) |> put_flash(:info, "User deleted")} {:error, _} -> {:noreply, put_flash(socket, :error, "Could not delete user")} end {:error, _} -> {:noreply, put_flash(socket, :error, "User not found")} end end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :refresh_insert, payload: %{user: user}}, socket) do {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :users, user, at: 0)} end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :refresh_update, payload: %{user: user}}, socket) do {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :users, user)} end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :refresh_delete, payload: %{user: user}}, socket) do {:noreply, stream_delete(socket, :users, user)} end # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Render — HEEx only. Uses ~p verified routes, <.link>, :for/:if directives, # phx-update="stream", to_form/2 inside <.form>, and <.live_component>. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- @impl true def render(assigns) do ~H"""

<%= @page_title %>

<.link navigate={~p"/users/new"} class="btn btn-primary"> New user
Loading stats...
Total: <%= @stats.result.total %> Active: <%= @stats.result.active %>
Failed to load stats.
<.live_component module={FilterPanelComponent} id="users-filter-panel" query={@query} sort_by={@sort_by} /> <.form for={to_form(%{"q" => @query}, as: :search)} phx-change="search" phx-submit="search" > <.input type="text" name="search[q]" value={@query} placeholder="Search users" />
<.link patch={~p"/users?sort=name"}>Name <.link patch={~p"/users?sort=email"}>Email Actions
<%= user.name %> <%= user.email %> <.link patch={~p"/users/#{user}/edit"}>Edit <.link phx-click="delete" phx-value-id={user.id} data-confirm="Are you sure?" > Delete
<.live_component :if={@live_action in [:new, :edit]} module={UserFormComponent} id={"user-form-#{(@selected_user && @selected_user.id) || "new"}"} action={@live_action} user={@selected_user || %User{}} return_to={~p"/users"} />
""" end end
supporting_files.references/anti-patterns.md:
# Phoenix 1.7+ LiveView — Anti-Patterns Catalog Every anti-pattern below ships with a mechanical detector (ripgrep or Credo) and a fix. Run the detectors top-to-bottom across any codebase to surface violations. --- ## 1. DB queries in `mount/3` **What it is**: Calling `Repo.all/1`, `Repo.get/2`, `Repo.one/1`, or `Repo.preload/2` directly inside `mount/3`. **Why it's bad**: `mount/3` runs twice on the initial page load — once over HTTP and once over the WebSocket. Every synchronous query inside it runs twice, doubling DB load and blocking first paint both times. **Detector**: ```bash rg -n 'def mount' -A 20 -g 'lib/**/*_live.ex' | rg -B1 'Repo\.(all|one|get|preload)' ``` **Bad**: ```elixir def mount(_params, _session, socket) do users = MyApp.Repo.all(MyApp.Accounts.User) {:ok, assign(socket, :users, users)} end ``` **Good**: ```elixir def mount(_params, _session, socket) do {:ok, assign_async(socket, :users, fn -> {:ok, %{users: Accounts.list_users()}} end)} end ``` **How to fix**: Wrap the query in `assign_async/3` so it runs in a Task after first paint, or guard with `if connected?(socket), do: load(), else: assign(socket, :items, [])` when the data is cheap enough that you'd rather skip the HTTP pass. --- ## 2. `PubSub.subscribe` without `connected?/1` guard **What it is**: Calling `Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe/2` unconditionally in `mount/3`. **Why it's bad**: Because `mount/3` runs twice (HTTP + WebSocket), an unguarded subscribe registers the process twice on first load, and on every reconnect the old WebSocket process is still subscribed — leaking processes and duplicating messages. **Detector**: ```bash rg -nP 'Phoenix\.PubSub\.subscribe' lib/ | rg -v 'connected\?' ``` **Bad**: ```elixir def mount(%{"id" => id}, _session, socket) do Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{id}") {:ok, assign(socket, :room_id, id)} end ``` **Good**: ```elixir def mount(%{"id" => id}, _session, socket) do if connected?(socket), do: Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{id}") {:ok, assign(socket, :room_id, id)} end ``` **How to fix**: Wrap the subscribe call in an `if connected?(socket)` guard. A custom Credo check (`Credo.Check.Warning.UnguardedPubSubSubscribe`) can enforce this across the codebase. --- ## 3. `Routes.*_path` / `Routes.*_url` leftovers **What it is**: Using pre-1.7 route helpers like `Routes.user_path(@socket, :show, id)` instead of the `~p` verified-route sigil. **Why it's bad**: Verified routes are mandatory in Phoenix 1.7+ — they validate routes at compile time, catch dead links during `mix compile`, and the old `Routes` module is generated only for backward compatibility. **Detector**: ```bash rg -n 'Routes\.\w+_(path|url)' lib/ test/ ``` **Bad**: ```elixir def handle_event("view", %{"id" => id}, socket) do {:noreply, push_navigate(socket, to: Routes.user_path(socket, :show, id))} end ``` **Good**: ```elixir def handle_event("view", %{"id" => id}, socket) do {:noreply, push_navigate(socket, to: ~p"/users/#{id}")} end ``` **How to fix**: Rewrite every `Routes.foo_path(...)` call as `~p"/..."`. The compiler will flag any route that doesn't actually exist in the router. --- ## 4. Bare `%Ecto.Changeset{}` passed to `<.form>` **What it is**: Passing a raw `%Ecto.Changeset{}` directly as `<.form for={@changeset}>` instead of wrapping it with `to_form/2`. **Why it's bad**: Skipping `to_form/2` means `<.form>` has to reconstruct form metadata on every render, breaks `<.inputs_for>` for nested associations, and produces incorrect `name` / `id` attributes on inputs. **Detector**: ```bash rg -nP '<\.form [^>]*for=\{@(?!.*to_form)' lib/ ``` **Bad**: ```heex <.form for={@changeset} phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save"> <.input field={@changeset[:email]} /> ``` **Good**: ```elixir form = Accounts.change_user(user) |> to_form() ``` ```heex <.form for={@form} phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save"> <.input field={@form[:email]} /> ``` **How to fix**: Always pipe the changeset through `to_form/2` before assigning it, and reference the form as `@form`. On validate errors, rebuild with `to_form(changeset, action: :validate)`. --- ## 5. `Enum` over assigns for lists >100 items **What it is**: Holding a large collection in a regular assign and iterating with `:for={item <- @items}`, instead of routing the collection through `stream/3`. **Why it's bad**: Every `@items` update sends the entire list over the wire and re-renders every row. Streams only send the diff (inserts/removes/replaces), keeping payload size and DOM churn bounded. **Detector**: ```bash rg -nP 'def list_\w+' lib/ -A 3 rg -nP ':for=\{[^}]+<-\s*@(?!streams\.)' -g 'lib/**/*.ex' -g 'lib/**/*.heex' ``` **Bad**: ```elixir def mount(_params, _session, socket) do {:ok, assign(socket, :messages, Chat.list_messages())} end ``` ```heex
<%= msg.body %>
``` **Good**: ```elixir def mount(_params, _session, socket) do {:ok, stream(socket, :messages, Chat.list_messages())} end ``` ```heex
<%= msg.body %>
``` **How to fix**: Replace `assign(socket, :messages, list)` with `stream(socket, :messages, list)`, wrap the iterating element in a parent with `phx-update="stream"`, and destructure `{dom_id, msg}` inside `:for` so every row carries its stream-assigned id. --- ## 6. `assign_new` for values needing fresh compute **What it is**: Using `assign_new(socket, :key, fn -> ... end)` for values like timestamps or DB reads that need to refresh on reconnect. **Why it's bad**: `assign_new/3` only runs the function when the key is missing from assigns. After a reconnect, the old value is still there — so the function never runs and the value goes stale. It's meant for caching values across the HTTP → WebSocket handoff, not for fresh compute. **Detector**: ```bash rg -nP 'assign_new.*fn ->' lib/ -A 3 | rg -B1 '(Repo|utc_now)' ``` **Bad**: ```elixir def mount(_params, _session, socket) do socket = assign_new(socket, :now, fn -> DateTime.utc_now() end) socket = assign_new(socket, :users, fn -> Accounts.list_users() end) {:ok, socket} end ``` **Good**: ```elixir def mount(_params, _session, socket) do {:ok, socket |> assign(:now, DateTime.utc_now()) |> assign_async(:users, fn -> {:ok, %{users: Accounts.list_users()}} end)} end ``` **How to fix**: Use plain `assign/3` for anything that must be fresh on each mount, and `assign_async/3` for DB reads. Reserve `assign_new/3` for values handed in from the `mount/3` plug pipeline (like `:current_user`). --- ## 7. Legacy navigation helpers (`live_link`, `live_patch`, `live_redirect`) **What it is**: Any use of `live_link`, `live_patch`, or `live_redirect` in a LiveView or HEEx template. **Why it's bad**: These helpers were removed in Phoenix 1.7. The replacement is `<.link navigate={...}>` / `<.link patch={...}>` for templates and `push_navigate/2` / `push_patch/2` for server-side navigation. **Detector**: ```bash rg -nP '\b(live_link|live_patch|live_redirect)\b' lib/ ``` **Bad**: ```heex <%= live_redirect "Edit", to: Routes.user_path(@socket, :edit, @user) %> <%= live_patch "Sort", to: "?sort=name" %> ``` **Good**: ```heex <.link navigate={~p"/users/#{@user}/edit"}>Edit <.link patch={~p"/users?sort=name"}>Sort ``` **How to fix**: Replace every legacy helper with `<.link>`. Use `navigate` when the target is a different LiveView, `patch` when it's the same LiveView and you want `handle_params/3` to pick up the change. --- ## 8. `<.live_component>` without `:id` **What it is**: Rendering a stateful `Phoenix.LiveComponent` without passing an `id` attribute. **Why it's bad**: LiveComponents require a stable `:id` to keep their state between renders. Without an id, LiveView treats the component as stateless — `update/2` runs fresh every render, `handle_event/3` callbacks can't route back to it, and every assign is lost on reassign. **Detector**: ```bash rg -n '<\.live_component' lib/ | rg -v 'id=' ``` **Bad**: ```heex <.live_component module={MyAppWeb.FilterPanel} query={@q} users={@users} /> ``` **Good**: ```heex <.live_component module={MyAppWeb.FilterPanel} id="filter-panel" query={@q} users={@users} /> ``` **How to fix**: Always pass a stable `id="..."` (not a random value) on every `<.live_component>`. If you render multiple instances, scope the id per instance (e.g. `id={"row-#{row.id}"}`). --- ## 9. `Repo` calls inside function components **What it is**: Calling `Repo.get/2`, `Repo.all/1`, or any context function that hits the database from inside a function component's body. **Why it's bad**: Function components are pure rendering primitives. They have no socket, no lifecycle, and run on every render — so a DB call inside one fires on every re-render (N+1 queries, at best). Data loading belongs in the parent LiveView or LiveComponent's `update/2`. **Detector**: ```bash rg -n 'def \w+\(assigns\)' -g 'lib/**/components*.ex' -A 20 | rg 'Repo\.' ``` **Bad**: ```elixir def user_card(assigns) do assigns = assign(assigns, :posts, MyApp.Repo.all(MyApp.Posts.for_user(assigns.user))) ~H"""

<%= @user.name %>

<%= length(@posts) %> posts
""" end ``` **Good**: ```elixir attr :user, :map, required: true attr :post_count, :integer, required: true def user_card(assigns) do ~H"""

<%= @user.name %>

<%= @post_count %> posts
""" end ``` **How to fix**: Move the DB call to the parent LiveView (or the LiveComponent's `update/2`), compute the derived value there, and pass it into the function component as a declared `attr`. --- ## 10. `PubSub.broadcast` called from `handle_event` **What it is**: Calling `Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast/3` directly inside a LiveView's `handle_event/3` clause, instead of routing the write through a context function that broadcasts. **Why it's bad**: Broadcasts should be context-owned so every caller — LiveView, Oban job, controller, test — fans out identically. Putting broadcasts in `handle_event` means only the LiveView path emits events; other writers silently diverge. **Detector**: ```bash rg -n 'def handle_event' -g 'lib/**/*_live.ex' -A 15 | rg 'PubSub\.broadcast' ``` **Bad**: ```elixir def handle_event("save", %{"message" => params}, socket) do {:ok, msg} = Chat.create_message(params) Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{msg.room_id}", {:new_message, msg}) {:noreply, socket} end ``` **Good**: ```elixir # in MyApp.Chat def create_message(attrs) do %Message{} |> Message.changeset(attrs) |> Repo.insert() |> broadcast(:message_created) end defp broadcast({:ok, %Message{room_id: id} = msg}, event) do Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{id}", {event, msg}) {:ok, msg} end defp broadcast({:error, _} = err, _event), do: err # in LiveView def handle_event("save", %{"message" => params}, socket) do {:ok, _} = Chat.create_message(params) {:noreply, socket} end ``` **How to fix**: Add a private `broadcast/2` helper to the context module, pipe every write through it, and remove all direct `PubSub.broadcast` calls from LiveViews. --- ## 11. Props assigned outside `update/2` (LiveComponent) **What it is**: Inside a LiveComponent, calling `assign(socket, :prop, assigns.prop)` from `handle_event/3` or `handle_info/2` instead of `update/2`. **Why it's bad**: `update/2` is the single choke point where parent-supplied props become socket assigns. Re-reading `assigns.prop` from another callback means the component is looking at stale prop values — `assigns.prop` in `handle_event` refers to the *event* payload, not the parent's assigns. **Detector**: ```bash rg -nU 'def handle_event[^\n]*\n[^}]*assign\(socket, :\w+, assigns\.' lib/ ``` **Bad**: ```elixir def handle_event("refresh", _params, socket) do {:noreply, assign(socket, :query, socket.assigns.query)} end def handle_event("sort", assigns, socket) do {:noreply, assign(socket, :sort, assigns.sort)} # assigns is event params, not props! end ``` **Good**: ```elixir def update(%{query: q, users: users} = assigns, socket) do visible = Enum.filter(users, &String.contains?(&1.name, q)) {:ok, socket |> assign(assigns) |> assign(:visible, visible)} end def handle_event("toggle", _params, socket) do {:noreply, assign(socket, :open?, not socket.assigns.open?)} end ``` **How to fix**: Move all prop-to-assign mapping into `update/2`. Keep `handle_event/3` strictly limited to component-owned UI state (`:open?`, `:selected_id`, etc.) and never re-read props from it. --- ## 12. Raw params map stuffed into `%Action{}` **What it is**: Creating an `%Action{}` struct whose `payload` field holds the raw LiveView event params map instead of coerced, typed fields. **Why it's bad**: The event-to-action funnel exists to make `handle_action/2` unit-testable with plain ExUnit. If the payload is a raw params map, every test needs to know the LiveView wire format, and type mismatches (strings vs atoms, nested maps) propagate into business logic. **Detector**: AST probe — look for `%Action{payload: params}` where `params` is the raw event-params binding from `handle_event`. ```bash rg -nP 'handle_event.*params.*\n.*%Action\{.*payload:\s*params' lib/ -U --multiline ``` **Bad**: ```elixir def handle_event("save", %{"user" => params}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :save, payload: params}, socket) end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :save, payload: params}, socket) do attrs = %{name: params["name"], email: params["email"]} # ... end ``` **Good**: ```elixir def handle_event("save", %{"user" => %{"name" => name, "email" => email}}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :save, payload: %{name: name, email: email}}, socket) end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :save, payload: %{name: name, email: email}}, socket) do case Accounts.create_user(%{name: name, email: email}) do {:ok, user} -> {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :users, user)} {:error, cs} -> {:noreply, assign(socket, :form, to_form(cs))} end end ``` **How to fix**: Pattern-match the event params at the `handle_event` boundary, coerce into typed fields, and build the `%Action{}` from those typed fields. `handle_action/2` should never see a string-keyed map. --- ## 13. Re-fetching a resource after `on_mount` scoped it **What it is**: After an `on_mount` hook has authorized and assigned a resource (e.g. `:post`), calling `Posts.get_post!/1` or `Repo.get/2` again inside `mount/3`. **Why it's bad**: Wastes a DB query and — worse — opens a TOCTOU gap. The hook did an authorized fetch scoped to the user; the re-fetch is unscoped and could return a record the user can't see, silently bypassing authz. **Detector**: ```bash rg -n 'on_mount.*:scope_' -g 'lib/**/*_live.ex' -A 30 | rg -E '(Posts\.get_post|Repo\.get)' ``` **Bad**: ```elixir # router hook already assigned :post def mount(%{"id" => id}, _session, socket) do post = MyApp.Posts.get_post!(id) # unscoped re-fetch {:ok, assign(socket, :post, post)} end ``` **Good**: ```elixir def mount(_params, _session, socket) do # trust socket.assigns.post from the on_mount hook {:ok, socket} end ``` **How to fix**: Trust what `on_mount` assigned. If you need the resource in a LiveComponent, pass it as a prop rather than letting the component re-fetch by id. --- ## 14. Template `<%= for %>` instead of `:for=` **What it is**: Using legacy EEx blocks like `<%= for item <- @items do %>...<% end %>` inside HEEx templates. **Why it's bad**: HEEx directives (`:for={...}`, `:if={...}`) participate in LiveView's diff tracking — they know which children changed and send minimal patches. EEx blocks bypass that optimization and re-render the entire block on every change. **Detector**: ```bash rg -nP '<%=\s*for\s+' -g 'lib/**/*.html.heex' -g 'lib/**/*_live.ex' ``` **Bad**: ```heex
    <%= for user <- @users do %>
  • <%= user.name %>
    • <% end %>
``` **Good**: ```heex
  • <%= user.name %>
``` **How to fix**: Rewrite every `<%= for %>` block as a `:for={...}` directive on the child element. The same holds for `<%= if %>` — use `:if={...}`. Always attach directives to the child being iterated, not a wrapping element.

# Phoenix 1.7+ LiveView — Cheatsheet ## Verified Routes (`~p` sigil) | Form | Example | |---|---| | Static | `~p"/users"` | | Interpolation | `~p"/users/#{id}"` | | Struct interpolation | `~p"/users/#{user}"` | | Query params | `~p"/users?sort=#{field}"` | | Multi-query | `~p"/users?#{[sort: field, dir: :asc]}"` | | Nested | `~p"/rooms/#{room}/messages/#{msg}"` | ## `<.link>` Attributes | Attr | Use for | Behavior | |---|---|---| | `navigate={~p"/x"}` | Cross-LiveView | Fresh mount, new process | | `patch={~p"/x"}` | Same LiveView URL update | Runs `handle_params/3` | | `href={~p"/x"}` | Non-LiveView page | Full browser reload | ```heex <.link navigate={~p"/users/#{user}"}>Show <.link patch={~p"/users?sort=name"}>Sort by name <.link href={~p"/logout"} method="delete">Log out ``` ## HEEx Directives ```heex
  • <%= user.name %>
    • ``` ## Function Components ```elixir attr :label, :string, required: true attr :variant, :atom, values: [:primary, :secondary], default: :primary attr :rest, :global, include: ~w(disabled form) slot :inner_block, required: true slot :actions def button(assigns) do ~H""" """ end ``` ## LiveComponent Lifecycle | Callback | Purpose | |---|---| | `update/2` | Single choke point for parent props → socket assigns | | `handle_event/3` | Mutate ONLY component-owned UI state | | `send_update/2` | Parent pushes new props by id: `send_update(MyComp, id: "x", prop: v)` | | `@myself` | Component's own target ref: `phx-click={JS.push("go", target: @myself)}` | ```heex <.live_component module={MyAppWeb.FilterPanel} id="filters" query={@q} users={@users} /> ``` ## Forms ```elixir form = Accounts.change_user(user) |> to_form() ``` ```heex <.form for={@form} phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save"> <.input field={@form[:email]} label="Email" type="email" /> <.inputs_for :let={cf} field={@form[:children]}> <.input field={cf[:name]} label="Name" /> ``` ## Streams ```elixir stream(socket, :messages, Chat.list_messages()) stream_insert(socket, :messages, msg, at: 0) stream_insert(socket, :messages, updated_msg) # same id = replace stream_delete(socket, :messages, msg) stream(socket, :messages, Chat.list_messages(), reset: true) ``` ```heex
    <%= msg.body %>
    ``` ## Mount + Lifecycle | Function | When it runs | What belongs here | |---|---|---| | `mount/3` | Once (HTTP + WS) | Non-blocking assigns, `assign_async` | | `handle_params/3` | After mount + on `patch` | URL-driven state, filters, sort | | `connected?/1` | In mount | Gate side effects: `if connected?(socket), do: ...` | ```elixir def mount(_params, _session, socket) do {:ok, socket |> assign_async(:users, fn -> {:ok, %{users: Accounts.list_users()}} end) |> assign_async(:stats, fn -> {:ok, %{stats: Accounts.summary()}} end)} end ``` ## Events | Callback | Source | Pattern | |---|---|---| | `handle_event/3` | Client: `phx-click`, `phx-submit` | `def handle_event("name", params, socket)` | | `handle_info/2` | Server: PubSub, `send/2` | `def handle_info({:tag, payload}, socket)` | | `push_event/3` | Server → JS hook | `push_event(socket, "scroll_to", %{id: 1})` | ## Navigation ```elixir {:noreply, push_navigate(socket, to: ~p"/users/#{id}")} # remount {:noreply, push_patch(socket, to: ~p"/users?sort=#{f}")} # same view ``` ```heex <.link navigate={~p"/users/#{user}"}>Open <.link patch={~p"/users?page=#{@page + 1}"}>Next ``` ## PubSub ```elixir def mount(_params, _session, socket) do if connected?(socket), do: Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{id}") {:ok, socket} end # Broadcasts go through the context, not handle_event defp broadcast({:ok, %Message{room_id: id} = msg}, event) do Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{id}", {event, msg}) {:ok, msg} end defp broadcast({:error, _} = err, _event), do: err ``` ## Auth + Authz (`on_mount`) ```elixir # router live_session :scoped, on_mount: [{MyAppWeb.UserAuth, :scope_post}] do live "/posts/:id", PostLive.Show end # hook def on_mount(:scope_post, %{"id" => id}, _session, socket) do case Posts.fetch_for_user(id, socket.assigns.current_user) do {:ok, post} -> {:cont, assign(socket, :post, post)} {:error, _} -> {:halt, socket |> put_flash(:error, "Not found") |> redirect(to: ~p"/")} end end ```

# Phoenix 1.7+ LiveView — Deep Reference Guide ## 1. Introduction Phoenix LiveView is a server-rendered, stateful UI library for the Phoenix web framework. A LiveView process holds the page state on the server, diffs its rendered template on every state change, and ships only the minimal HTML patch over a persistent WebSocket. The result feels like a Single-Page Application to the user but keeps the programming model close to traditional server-side Phoenix: controllers become `LiveView` modules, helpers become `function components`, and JavaScript is opt-in rather than mandatory. This guide is the deep reference for the `elixir-phoenix-liveview-composite` skill. It assumes you already know why LiveView exists and want to look up specific patterns while implementing or reviewing 1.7+ code. The top-level `SKILL.md` contains the rules and anti-patterns; this document contains the "why" and the worked examples. **Version matrix this guide targets:** Elixir ~> 1.15 Erlang/OTP ~> 26 Phoenix ~> 1.7.10 Phoenix LiveView ~> 0.20 (or the 1.0 RC — the 1.7+ idioms are the same) Ecto ~> 3.11 **Phoenix 1.6 is explicitly out of scope.** If you see `live_link`, `live_patch`, `live_redirect`, `Routes.user_path`, or `<%= for %>` in HEEx, you are reading a pre-1.7 codebase and should migrate before applying the patterns here. **Authoritative upstream docs this guide complements:** - `https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix_live_view/` — the official LiveView docs - `https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix/` — Phoenix framework - `https://hexdocs.pm/ecto/Ecto.Changeset.html#cast_assoc/3` — cast_assoc - `https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix_live_view/Phoenix.LiveView.html#assign_async/3` — assign_async The rest of this guide is organized by topic, with cross-references (see §N.M) between closely related sections. Every section includes at least one full, runnable Elixir code block. ## 2. Architectural principles The central rule: **every LiveView starts as a thin parent coordinator, and every piece of stateful UI gets pushed into a LiveComponent**. The parent owns exactly three things: URL state (read in `handle_params/3`), session data (the current user), and PubSub subscriptions. Everything else — forms, filter panels, modals, sortable lists, editable rows — lives inside a `Phoenix.LiveComponent` with its own `:id`, its own assigns, and its own `handle_event/3`. **Why this shape?** Three reasons, in order of importance: 1. **Re-render isolation.** When a LiveComponent's socket mutates, only that component's fragment re-renders. If the parent owned the same state, every assign change would bubble the whole page through a diff pass. A chat window with 200 messages and an editable profile form in the sidebar should NOT re-render the chat feed when the user types in the name input. 2. **Testability.** A LiveComponent is a self-contained unit you can render with `render_component/2` and exercise with a small `LiveViewTest` harness. A monolithic parent LiveView with 15 `handle_event` clauses is a nightmare to unit-test. 3. **Cognitive load.** Each file stays under ~200 lines with one clear responsibility. Grep for the component name in the templates and you know where to look. ### When to use LiveView vs LiveComponent vs function component Use a **function component** when the thing you are rendering has no state of its own, takes all its inputs as attrs, and produces HEEx. Buttons, badges, cards, form inputs, alerts. Stateless. No socket. See §4. Use a **LiveComponent** when you have a discrete interactive region that owns state: a multi-step form, a sortable list, a filterable panel, a modal. The parent passes props in via `assigns`, and the component mutates its own socket via `handle_event/3`. See §5. Use a **LiveView** only at the top level — one per route. The LiveView is the process. It owns the URL, the session, and coordinates child LiveComponents. You should almost never have a LiveView nested inside another LiveView (that's what LiveComponents are for). ### The "thin parent, stateful children" rule — worked example Consider a page at `/teams/:id` with a team header, an editable member list, a filter panel, and an add-member modal. The wrong shape: # BAD: everything in the parent defmodule MyAppWeb.TeamLive do def mount(_, _, socket) do {:ok, assign(socket, team: nil, members: [], filter: "", modal_open?: false, form: nil)} end def handle_event("filter", %{"q" => q}, socket), do: ... def handle_event("open_modal", _, socket), do: ... def handle_event("add_member", %{"member" => attrs}, socket), do: ... def handle_event("remove_member", %{"id" => id}, socket), do: ... def handle_event("validate", %{"member" => attrs}, socket), do: ... # ...12 more clauses end The right shape: defmodule MyAppWeb.TeamLive do use MyAppWeb, :live_view def mount(%{"id" => id}, _session, socket) do {:ok, socket |> assign_async(:team, fn -> {:ok, %{team: MyApp.Teams.get!(id)}} end)} end def render(assigns) do ~H"""
<.live_component module={TeamHeader} id={"team-#{@team.result.id}"} team={@team.result} /> <.live_component module={MemberList} id={"members-#{@team.result.id}"} team={@team.result} /> <.live_component module={AddMemberModal} id="add-member-modal" team={@team.result} />
""" end end Each child is its own `Phoenix.LiveComponent` with focused state and a single responsibility. See §5 for the discipline inside each component. ## 3. HEEx template reference HEEx is Phoenix's typed, compile-time-validated template language. The `~H` sigil parses the template at compile time and raises on malformed tags, unknown components, or unresolved attrs. ### 3.1 Verified routes with `~p` Every internal route uses the `~p` sigil. The router's `MyAppWeb.Router` compiles a set of allowed paths at build time; any `~p"/typo"` that doesn't match a declared route raises a compile-time warning. # Static route ~p"/users" # With a path param — interpolated value is URL-encoded automatically ~p"/users/#{@user}" # implements String.Chars or Phoenix.Param ~p"/users/#{@user.id}" # explicit id # With query params as a map ~p"/users?#{[sort: "name", page: 2]}" # renders: /users?sort=name&page=2 # Merging query params from an existing map params = %{sort: "name", status: "active"} ~p"/users?#{params}" # Query params can also be a keyword list ~p"/search?#{[q: "phoenix", limit: 20]}" The interpolation respects `Phoenix.Param`, so passing a struct (like `%User{id: 3}`) resolves to the right path segment. This is why you can write `~p"/users/#{@user}"` and not `~p"/users/#{@user.id}"`. ### 3.2 HEEx directives — `:for` and `:if` Replace legacy `<%= for %>` / `<%= if %>` with native directives:
  • <%= user.name %>
Hello, <%= @current_user.name %>
Directives can combine: <%= user.name %> # With a block form for nested iteration + conditional

<%= group.name %>

<%= member.name %>

The directive form is strictly more optimized than `<%= for %>`. LiveView's change-tracker can diff the iterated list item-by-item; the Eex form forces a full re-render of the surrounding block. ### 3.3 Class lists HEEx supports a class list literal — a list of `{condition, class}` tuples, or a mix of strings and lists:
Click me
Falsy entries (`nil`, `false`) are filtered out. No more string concatenation or `Enum.join`. ### 3.4 The `<.link>` component `<.link>` is the universal navigation primitive, replacing `live_link`, `live_patch`, `live_redirect`, and raw `` for internal routes. # Cross-LiveView navigation (old live_redirect/live_link) <.link navigate={~p"/users/#{@user}"}>View user # Same-LiveView URL patch (old live_patch) <.link patch={~p"/users?sort=#{@sort}"}>Sort # External link or full-page reload <.link href="https://example.com">External <.link href={~p"/logout"} method="delete">Log out See §11 for the full navigate-vs-patch decision tree. ### 3.5 The `~H` sigil Every template body inside a module uses `~H`, not `~E` or `~h`: def render(assigns) do ~H"""

Hello, <%= @name %>

""" end The triple-quoted heredoc is the idiomatic form. `~H` is what enables compile-time validation — a typo in an attr name or a missing component will raise during `mix compile`, not at runtime. ## 4. Function components + slots A function component is a plain Elixir function with a single `assigns` argument that returns HEEx. Its job is to render. It has no socket, no database, no `case` statements on business logic. ### 4.1 `attr` declarations Every input to a function component must be declared with `attr`. This gives you compile-time validation, IDE hints, and documentation. attr :name, :string, required: true attr :variant, :atom, values: [:primary, :secondary, :danger], default: :primary attr :disabled, :boolean, default: false attr :rest, :global, include: ~w(form name) def button(assigns) do ~H""" """ end Key attr options: - `:required` — raises at compile time if omitted - `:default` — used when the caller doesn't pass the attr - `:values` — an enumerated constraint (compile-time checked against literal attrs) - `:rest, :global` — collects undeclared attrs into a map, passes to HTML via `{@rest}`. The `include:` list whitelists additional global attrs like `form=` or `name=`. ### 4.2 Slots Slots are named child-content regions. Every function component gets a free `:inner_block` slot; declare additional slots explicitly. slot :inner_block, required: true slot :actions slot :footer def card(assigns) do ~H"""
<%= render_slot(@inner_block) %>
<%= render_slot(@actions) %>
<%= render_slot(@footer) %>
""" end Usage: <.card>

Main content here.

<:actions> <.button name="Save" /> <.button name="Cancel" variant={:secondary} /> <:footer> Last updated <%= @updated_at %> ### 4.3 `:let` — passing data from the component to the slot When the component wants to parameterize a slot (e.g., a table row renderer that needs the row data), use `:let`: slot :col, required: true do attr :label, :string, required: true end attr :rows, :list, required: true def table(assigns) do ~H"""
<%= col.label %>
<%= render_slot(col, row) %>
""" end Usage — the caller receives each row via `:let={row}`: <.table rows={@users}> <:col :let={user} label="Name"><%= user.name %> <:col :let={user} label="Email"><%= user.email %> <:col :let={user} label="Actions"> <.link navigate={~p"/users/#{user}"}>View ### 4.4 Worked example — polymorphic card attr :title, :string, required: true attr :variant, :atom, values: [:info, :warning, :danger, :success], default: :info attr :icon, :string, default: nil slot :inner_block, required: true slot :footer def alert_card(assigns) do ~H"""

<%= @title %>

<%= render_slot(@inner_block) %>
<%= render_slot(@footer) %>
""" end If a decision is needed — e.g., a badge color for a status — **compute it in the parent**, not in the component: # BAD — branching inside the function component def status_badge(assigns) do ~H""" "bg-green" :pending -> "bg-yellow" :inactive -> "bg-gray" end }> <%= @status %> """ end # GOOD — parent computes the class, passes as attr def mount(_, _, socket) do {:ok, assign(socket, :badge_class, badge_class_for(user.status))} end # In the component: attr :class, :string, required: true attr :label, :string, required: true def status_badge(assigns) do ~H""" <%= @label %> """ end ## 5. LiveComponents A `Phoenix.LiveComponent` is a stateful child of a LiveView. It shares the parent LiveView's process, but has its own socket assigns, its own `handle_event/3`, and its own render function. The LiveView process owns the component's state, but the component has exclusive write access to it. ### 5.1 The `update/2` discipline This is the most important rule in this section, and the easiest to get wrong. **`update/2` is the ONLY function that calls `assign(socket, :prop, assigns.prop)` for external data.** If you assign a prop somewhere else — `handle_event`, `handle_info`, a helper function — you break the component's lifecycle contract. Why? When the parent LiveView re-renders, LiveView calls `update/2` on every child LiveComponent with the new assigns. If you need any derived state (a filtered list, a computed label, a sorted index), compute it right there, in the same pass. Later mutations by the component itself (toggling `:open?`, selecting a row) happen in `handle_event`, and they only touch component-local state — never the props. defmodule MyAppWeb.FilterPanel do use MyAppWeb, :live_component def update(%{users: users, query: q} = assigns, socket) do visible = Enum.filter(users, &String.contains?(&1.name, q)) {:ok, socket |> assign(assigns) |> assign(:visible, visible) |> assign_new(:open?, fn -> false end)} end def handle_event("toggle", _, socket) do {:noreply, assign(socket, :open?, not socket.assigns.open?)} end def render(assigns) do ~H"""
  • <%= user.name %>
""" end end Note `assign_new(:open?, fn -> false end)` — this sets a default only on first mount so subsequent re-renders don't clobber the user's toggle state. ### 5.2 `phx-target={@myself}` — routing events to the component By default, `phx-click="toggle"` sends the event to the parent LiveView. To route it to the component itself, add `phx-target={@myself}`. The `@myself` assign is a magic assign LiveView injects into every component — a CID (component id) that LiveView uses internally. Without `phx-target={@myself}`, the parent's `handle_event/3` runs, and the component never sees the event. ### 5.3 `send_update/2` — parent pushing into a child When the parent needs to push a prop change mid-lifecycle, call `send_update/2`. This causes LiveView to invoke the child's `update/2` with the new assigns merged in. # In the parent LiveView's handle_info/2: def handle_info({:new_user, user}, socket) do send_update(MyAppWeb.UserList, id: "user-list-main", new_user: user) {:noreply, socket} end Important: `send_update/2` is partial — the assigns you pass are merged into the existing ones. If you want to replace a list entirely, pass the whole new list. ### 5.4 Child-to-parent signaling LiveComponents cannot directly return a message to the parent. Instead, send a message to `self()` (which is the LiveView's process) and handle it in the parent's `handle_info/2`: # In the component: def handle_event("selected", %{"id" => id}, socket) do send(self(), {:user_selected, id}) {:noreply, socket} end # In the parent LiveView: def handle_info({:user_selected, id}, socket) do {:noreply, push_patch(socket, to: ~p"/users/#{id}")} end Include `@myself` in the message if the parent needs to reply via `send_update/2`: send(self(), {:toggle_sidebar, @myself}) ### 5.5 The `:id` requirement Every `<.live_component />` call **must include `id:`**. Without it, the component becomes stateless — LiveView rebuilds its assigns from scratch on every parent render, and `handle_event` state vanishes between interactions. # CORRECT <.live_component module={MyAppWeb.FilterPanel} id="filter-main" users={@users} query={@q} /> # BROKEN — no id, state resets on every parent render <.live_component module={MyAppWeb.FilterPanel} users={@users} query={@q} /> Use a stable id — derive it from the resource id (`"user-form-#{user.id}"`) or use a constant string if there's only one instance on the page (`"filter-main"`). ## 6. Forms + nested associations Forms in Phoenix 1.7+ always flow through `Phoenix.Component.to_form/2`. You build a changeset, wrap it in a form struct, assign it to the socket, and pass it to the `<.form>` component. For nested associations (parent with many children that can be added and removed dynamically), use `cast_assoc` with `:sort_param` and `:drop_param`. ### 6.1 Simple form # In the context: def change_user(user, attrs \\ %{}) do User.changeset(user, attrs) end # In the LiveComponent: def update(%{user: user} = assigns, socket) do form = Accounts.change_user(user) |> to_form() {:ok, socket |> assign(assigns) |> assign(:form, form)} end def handle_event("validate", %{"user" => params}, socket) do changeset = socket.assigns.user |> Accounts.change_user(params) |> Map.put(:action, :validate) {:noreply, assign(socket, :form, to_form(changeset))} end def handle_event("save", %{"user" => params}, socket) do case Accounts.update_user(socket.assigns.user, params) do {:ok, user} -> send(self(), {:user_updated, user}) {:noreply, assign(socket, :form, Accounts.change_user(user) |> to_form())} {:error, changeset} -> {:noreply, assign(socket, :form, to_form(changeset))} end end def render(assigns) do ~H""" <.form for={@form} phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save" phx-target={@myself}> <.input field={@form[:name]} label="Name" /> <.input field={@form[:email]} type="email" label="Email" /> <.button type="submit">Save """ end ### 6.2 Nested has_many — the `cast_assoc` pattern Consider a `Team` that has_many `Members`, and you want a single form that creates or edits the team and all its members at once, with add/remove buttons for each member row. # Schema: schema "teams" do field :name, :string has_many :members, Member, on_replace: :delete timestamps() end def changeset(team, attrs) do team |> cast(attrs, [:name]) |> validate_required([:name]) |> cast_assoc(:members, with: &Member.changeset/2, sort_param: :members_sort, drop_param: :members_drop ) end The `:sort_param` and `:drop_param` options are what makes dynamic add/remove work without manual index juggling. Ecto reads a `members_sort` array from the params (each element is either a numeric index into the existing rows or `"new"` for a fresh row) and builds the new collection from that. The `members_drop` array lists indices to delete. ### 6.3 Template — parent form with dynamic child rows <.form for={@form} phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save" phx-target={@myself}> <.input field={@form[:name]} label="Team name" />

Members

<.inputs_for :let={mf} field={@form[:members]}>
<.input field={mf[:name]} label="Name" /> <.input field={mf[:email]} label="Email" />
<.button type="submit">Save team The trailing `` ensures the `members_drop` key is always present in params, even if no row is being removed — otherwise `cast_assoc` can't tell the difference between "nothing dropped" and "drop all". ### 6.4 Why put the form in a LiveComponent? Lifting a complex nested form into the parent LiveView is tempting but wrong. See §2. The form owns its own state — changeset, form struct, error messages, the currently-focused row — and the parent doesn't need to re-render every time the user types. Create a `TeamFormComponent`, pass the team in as a prop, and let it own `handle_event("validate")` and `handle_event("save")`. The parent just receives `{:team_saved, team}` via `send(self(), ...)` and navigates. ## 7. Streams and collections `Phoenix.LiveView.stream/3` is the right choice for any collection over ~100 items or any list that grows via realtime updates. Streams live under `@streams.` (not regular assigns) and LiveView manages their DOM identity so individual inserts and deletes don't force a full re-render. ### 7.1 Basic stream def mount(_params, _session, socket) do {:ok, stream(socket, :messages, Chat.list_messages())} end def render(assigns) do ~H"""
<%= msg.body %>
""" end Note the two required bits: 1. The outer container has `id="messages"` and `phx-update="stream"`. 2. Each child element has `id={dom_id}` where `dom_id` is the first element of the `{dom_id, msg}` tuple. Without these, the stream machinery can't track which DOM nodes correspond to which stream items. ### 7.2 Mutating a stream def handle_info({:new_message, msg}, socket) do {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :messages, msg, at: 0)} end def handle_info({:message_updated, msg}, socket) do {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :messages, msg)} # same id -> update in place end def handle_info({:message_deleted, msg}, socket) do {:noreply, stream_delete(socket, :messages, msg)} end `stream_insert/3` uses the item's `id` field (or whatever's configured via `:dom_id` in the initial `stream/3` call) to decide whether to insert a new node or replace an existing one. The `at: 0` option prepends the new row. ### 7.3 Reset on filter / sort / search When the entire collection changes (user applied a filter, sorted a column, typed in a search box), you cannot diff — LiveView has no way to know which old rows should disappear and which new rows are updates. Use `reset: true`: def handle_params(params, _uri, socket) do items = Catalog.list_items(params) {:noreply, stream(socket, :items, items, reset: true)} end The `reset: true` option clears the stream's internal state and replaces everything in one pass. Use it whenever the collection identity changes. ### 7.4 Stream vs assign — when to use which - **Use stream** for: collections over ~100 items, realtime feeds, chat messages, notifications, audit logs, large tables, anything that grows. - **Use assign** for: small fixed lists (nav items, tabs, category filters, dropdown options), anything under ~50 items, anything you need to `Enum.count/1` or `Enum.find/2` on. Streams are opaque — you cannot `Enum.count/1` `@streams.messages`. Track the length in a separate assign (`assign(socket, :message_count, n)`) if you need it in the template. ## 8. Mount + lifecycle A LiveView process has two phases on first load: 1. **HTTP mount.** The LiveView runs `mount/3` and `render/1` synchronously over HTTP, sends the rendered HTML back, and terminates the HTTP process. The user sees the page. 2. **WebSocket mount.** The browser opens a WebSocket connection, and the LiveView runs `mount/3` AGAIN as a long-lived process. The socket is now `connected?(socket) == true`. **This means `mount/3` runs twice.** Any database query in mount runs twice on first page load — once over HTTP, once over WebSocket. This is the #1 source of performance bugs in LiveView. ### 8.1 The `connected?(socket)` guard For side effects that should only happen once (PubSub subscriptions, periodic timers, analytics tracking), guard with `connected?(socket)`: def mount(%{"id" => id}, _session, socket) do if connected?(socket) do Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{id}") :timer.send_interval(5_000, :tick) end {:ok, assign(socket, :room_id, id)} end ### 8.2 `handle_params/3` vs `mount/3` Put data-loading logic in `handle_params/3`, not `mount/3`. `handle_params/3` runs on initial mount AND on every `push_patch`. If the user patches from `/users?sort=name` to `/users?sort=email`, `handle_params/3` fires and can re-query with the new sort. `mount/3` does not fire again. def mount(_params, _session, socket) do {:ok, socket} end def handle_params(params, _uri, socket) do sort = params["sort"] || "name" users = Accounts.list_users(sort: sort) {:noreply, assign(socket, users: users, sort: sort)} end ### 8.3 `assign_async/3` for non-blocking loads `assign_async/3` (LiveView 0.20+) runs a function in a Task, renders a loading state immediately, and resolves the assign when the task finishes. The template branches on three flags: `@users.loading`, `@users.ok?`, `@users.failed`. def mount(_params, _session, socket) do {:ok, socket |> assign_async(:users, fn -> {:ok, %{users: Accounts.list_users()}} end) |> assign_async(:stats, fn -> {:ok, %{stats: Accounts.summary()}} end)} end def render(assigns) do ~H"""
Loading users...
Failed to load users.
<.user_list :if={@users.ok?} users={@users.result} />
Loading stats...
<.stats_panel :if={@stats.ok?} stats={@stats.result} /> """ end Each `assign_async/3` resolves on its own timeline — users load fast, stats load slowly, the user sees each section populate independently. ### 8.4 Gotcha — don't close over the socket Bad: def mount(_, _, socket) do {:ok, assign_async(socket, :user, fn -> id = socket.assigns.current_user.id # closes over entire socket! {:ok, %{user: Accounts.get!(id)}} end)} end The closure captures the entire socket — a huge map. It gets copied into the Task process, wasting memory and risking stale references. Extract the primitives first: def mount(_, _, socket) do user_id = socket.assigns.current_user.id {:ok, assign_async(socket, :user, fn -> {:ok, %{user: Accounts.get!(user_id)}} end)} end ## 9. Event handling Phoenix LiveView has two callback families for events: - **`handle_event/3`** — user-initiated events from the browser (`phx-click`, `phx-change`, `phx-submit`, `phx-keydown`). - **`handle_info/2`** — process-level messages (PubSub broadcasts, `send/2` calls, timer ticks). Both can mutate the socket. Without discipline, state-change logic gets scattered across a dozen clauses that each do a little bit of parsing, a little bit of querying, a little bit of assigning. ### 9.1 The event-to-action funnel Route every state change through a single private function called `handle_action/2`. Each `handle_event/3` clause does nothing but parse the params into a typed struct and delegate. defmodule MyAppWeb.RoomLive do use MyAppWeb, :live_view defmodule Action do defstruct [:type, :payload] end def handle_event("save", %{"message" => %{"body" => body}}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :save, payload: %{body: body}}, socket) end def handle_event("delete", %{"id" => id}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :delete, payload: %{id: String.to_integer(id)}}, socket) end def handle_info({:new_message, msg}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :received, payload: %{message: msg}}, socket) end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :save, payload: %{body: body}}, socket) do case Chat.create_message(%{body: body, room_id: socket.assigns.room_id}) do {:ok, _msg} -> {:noreply, socket} {:error, cs} -> {:noreply, assign(socket, :form, to_form(cs))} end end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :delete, payload: %{id: id}}, socket) do Chat.delete_message(id) {:noreply, socket} end defp handle_action(%Action{type: :received, payload: %{message: msg}}, socket) do {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :messages, msg, at: 0)} end end ### 9.2 Why this pattern - **Unit testable.** `handle_action/2` takes a socket-like struct and an Action and returns `{:noreply, socket}`. You can test it with plain ExUnit — no `LiveViewTest`, no `conn |> live(...)` boilerplate. - **One place to look.** All state transitions live in `handle_action/2`. Reading a LiveView module becomes: "parse params here, dispatch here, business logic here". - **Consistent between browser and server events.** `handle_event` and `handle_info` both funnel into the same dispatcher. PubSub broadcasts and user clicks get the same treatment. ### 9.3 When to split Keep `handle_action/2` under ~10 clauses per module. Beyond that, split the LiveView — usually by pushing a related cluster of actions into a dedicated LiveComponent. See §2. ## 10. PubSub + realtime Phoenix.PubSub is the message bus for fanning out events across processes. LiveView uses it for realtime updates: user A posts a message, the context broadcasts, every subscribed LiveView for that chat room gets the message in its `handle_info/2`, and each one's template updates via `stream_insert`. ### 10.1 Context-owned broadcast — the single-source rule Every broadcast lives inside the Ecto context next to the write that triggered it. Not inside `handle_event/3`, not in a controller, not in an Oban job. One function, one side effect, one broadcast. defmodule MyApp.Chat do alias MyApp.Chat.Message alias MyApp.Repo def create_message(attrs) do %Message{} |> Message.changeset(attrs) |> Repo.insert() |> broadcast(:message_created) end def update_message(%Message{} = msg, attrs) do msg |> Message.changeset(attrs) |> Repo.update() |> broadcast(:message_updated) end def delete_message(%Message{} = msg) do msg |> Repo.delete() |> broadcast(:message_deleted) end defp broadcast({:ok, %Message{room_id: id} = msg}, event) do Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{id}", {event, msg}) {:ok, msg} end defp broadcast({:error, _} = err, _event), do: err end The `defp broadcast/2` helper takes an `{:ok, result}` tuple and fans out. The `{:error, _}` clause short-circuits — never broadcast on failure. ### 10.2 Subscribing in LiveView In the parent LiveView's `mount/3`, subscribe only when connected: def mount(%{"id" => id}, _session, socket) do if connected?(socket) do Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{id}") end {:ok, socket |> assign(:room_id, id) |> stream(:messages, Chat.list_messages(id))} end def handle_info({:message_created, msg}, socket) do {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :messages, msg, at: 0)} end def handle_info({:message_updated, msg}, socket) do {:noreply, stream_insert(socket, :messages, msg)} end def handle_info({:message_deleted, msg}, socket) do {:noreply, stream_delete(socket, :messages, msg)} end ### 10.3 Topic naming Topics are strings. Keep them consistent across the app: - Per-resource: `"room:123"`, `"post:42"`, `"user:#{user.id}"` - Per-scope: `"rooms"`, `"notifications"` - Per-user-per-resource: `"user:123:rooms"` for private broadcasts Prefer underscored prefixes for domain isolation in larger apps: `"chat:room:123"`. ### 10.4 Routing broadcasts into LiveComponents When a PubSub message arrives at the parent LiveView but the state lives in a child LiveComponent, use `send_update/2` to push it into the child: def handle_info({:member_added, member}, socket) do send_update(MyAppWeb.MemberList, id: "members-#{socket.assigns.team.id}", new_member: member) {:noreply, socket} end ### 10.5 Phoenix.Presence basics `Phoenix.Presence` tracks who's online in a topic. It's built on PubSub and CRDTs, so it handles node joins and partitions gracefully. defmodule MyAppWeb.Presence do use Phoenix.Presence, otp_app: :my_app, pubsub_server: MyApp.PubSub end # In LiveView mount: def mount(%{"id" => id}, %{"user_id" => user_id}, socket) do if connected?(socket) do Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(MyApp.PubSub, "room:#{id}") {:ok, _} = MyAppWeb.Presence.track(self(), "room:#{id}", user_id, %{}) end {:ok, assign(socket, :present, MyAppWeb.Presence.list("room:#{id}"))} end def handle_info(%{event: "presence_diff"}, socket) do {:noreply, assign(socket, :present, MyAppWeb.Presence.list("room:#{socket.assigns.room_id}"))} end ## 11. Navigation patterns LiveView has three navigation primitives, and choosing the wrong one is the second most common bug (after DB queries in mount). ### 11.1 Decision tree - **Same LiveView module, URL change?** → `push_patch` / `<.link patch={}>`. Fires `handle_params/3` in the current LiveView. No remount. - **Different LiveView module?** → `push_navigate` / `<.link navigate={}>`. Unmounts the current LiveView, mounts the new one fresh. - **Full page reload or external URL?** → `<.link href={}>`. Leaves LiveView entirely. ### 11.2 `<.link>` with both attrs # Same-LiveView sort change — patch <.link patch={~p"/users?sort=name"}>Sort by name # Cross-LiveView drill-down — navigate <.link navigate={~p"/users/#{user}"}>View user # External or reload <.link href="https://docs.example.com">Docs <.link href={~p"/logout"} method="delete">Log out ### 11.3 Imperative navigation in event handlers def handle_event("sort", %{"by" => field}, socket) do {:noreply, push_patch(socket, to: ~p"/users?sort=#{field}")} end def handle_event("view", %{"id" => id}, socket) do {:noreply, push_navigate(socket, to: ~p"/users/#{id}")} end def handle_params(%{"sort" => field}, _uri, socket) do users = Accounts.list_users(sort: field) {:noreply, assign(socket, users: users, sort: field)} end ### 11.4 URL-is-state philosophy The URL is the single source of truth for view state. Filter values, selected tab, current page, sort column — all of them live in the query string, not in assigns. `handle_params/3` derives assigns from the URL. Any user interaction that changes view state calls `push_patch` with the new params, which fires `handle_params/3`, which re-derives assigns. Round trip. Clean. def handle_event("filter", %{"q" => q}, socket) do new_params = socket.assigns.params |> Map.put("q", q) |> Map.put("page", "1") {:noreply, push_patch(socket, to: ~p"/things?#{new_params}")} end ### 11.5 Return-to after login A common pattern: user clicks a link that requires auth, gets redirected to login, logs in, returns to the original page. # Before redirecting to login: socket = put_session(socket, :return_to, current_path(socket)) {:noreply, push_navigate(socket, to: ~p"/login")} # After successful login: return_to = get_session(socket, :return_to) || ~p"/" {:noreply, push_navigate(socket, to: return_to)} ## 12. Auth + authorization LiveView authentication and per-resource authorization both live in `on_mount` hooks, wired via `live_session` in the router. The hook runs before `mount/3`; if auth fails, the hook returns `{:halt, socket |> redirect(...)}` and `mount/3` never runs. ### 12.1 The `on_mount` hook defmodule MyAppWeb.UserAuth do import Phoenix.LiveView import Phoenix.Component def on_mount(:ensure_authenticated, _params, session, socket) do case session do %{"user_id" => user_id} -> user = MyApp.Accounts.get_user!(user_id) {:cont, assign(socket, :current_user, user)} _ -> {:halt, socket |> put_flash(:error, "You must log in to access this page.") |> redirect(to: ~p"/login")} end end def on_mount(:scope_post, %{"id" => id}, _session, socket) do user = socket.assigns.current_user case MyApp.Posts.fetch_for_user(id, user) do {:ok, post} -> {:cont, assign(socket, :post, post)} {:error, :not_found} -> {:halt, socket |> put_flash(:error, "Post not found.") |> redirect(to: ~p"/posts")} {:error, :forbidden} -> {:halt, socket |> put_flash(:error, "You don't have access to that post.") |> redirect(to: ~p"/")} end end end ### 12.2 Router wiring scope "/", MyAppWeb do pipe_through :browser live_session :public, on_mount: [] do live "/", HomeLive end live_session :authenticated, on_mount: [{MyAppWeb.UserAuth, :ensure_authenticated}] do live "/dashboard", DashboardLive end live_session :post_scoped, on_mount: [ {MyAppWeb.UserAuth, :ensure_authenticated}, {MyAppWeb.UserAuth, :scope_post} ] do live "/posts/:id/edit", PostEditLive end end ### 12.3 Context-layer authz decision The context function `Posts.fetch_for_user/2` is where the real authz decision happens. It takes the user AND the resource id, and returns `{:ok, post}` only if the user is authorized to access it. defmodule MyApp.Posts do def fetch_for_user(id, %User{role: :admin}) do case Repo.get(Post, id) do nil -> {:error, :not_found} post -> {:ok, post} end end def fetch_for_user(id, %User{id: user_id}) do case Repo.get_by(Post, id: id, user_id: user_id) do nil -> # Distinguish "not found" from "not yours" carefully: case Repo.get(Post, id) do nil -> {:error, :not_found} _ -> {:error, :forbidden} end post -> {:ok, post} end end end ### 12.4 Defense in depth Even though the on_mount hook authorized access, mutations still take the user as an argument at the context layer: def update_post(%Post{} = post, attrs, %User{} = user) do if can_edit?(post, user) do post |> Post.changeset(attrs) |> Repo.update() else {:error, :forbidden} end end This guards against bugs where a LiveComponent accidentally exposes a mutation path that bypasses the hook (e.g., via a PubSub message handler). ## 13. Testing with LiveViewTest `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` gives you synchronous, in-process testing of LiveViews without booting a browser. ### 13.1 Mounting and asserting import Phoenix.LiveViewTest test "renders the users page", %{conn: conn} do {:ok, view, html} = live(conn, ~p"/users") assert html =~ "Users" assert has_element?(view, "h1", "Users") assert has_element?(view, "a[href='/users/new']", "New user") end `live/2` returns `{:ok, view, html}`. `view` is the LiveViewTest handle, `html` is the initial rendered HTML. Use `html =~ "text"` for string contains, `has_element?/3` for selector-based assertions. ### 13.2 Triggering events test "clicking sort patches the URL", %{conn: conn} do {:ok, view, _html} = live(conn, ~p"/users") view |> element("a", "Sort by name") |> render_click() assert_patched(view, ~p"/users?sort=name") assert has_element?(view, "tr:first-child td", "Alice") end ### 13.3 Form submission test "creating a user", %{conn: conn} do {:ok, view, _html} = live(conn, ~p"/users/new") view |> form("#user-form", user: %{name: "Bob", email: "bob@example.com"}) |> render_submit() assert_redirect(view, ~p"/users") end ### 13.4 Stream assertions test "new message appears in the stream", %{conn: conn} do {:ok, view, _html} = live(conn, ~p"/rooms/123") send(view.pid, {:new_message, %Message{id: 1, body: "hello"}}) assert render(view) =~ "hello" assert has_element?(view, "#messages div[id^='messages-']", "hello") end ## 14. Anti-patterns cheatsheet This is the rapid-reference form of the composite skill's anti-pattern catalog. Every entry has a bad example and a good example. ### 14.1 DB queries in mount/3 # BAD def mount(_, _, socket) do {:ok, assign(socket, :users, Repo.all(User))} end # GOOD def mount(_, _, socket) do {:ok, assign_async(socket, :users, fn -> {:ok, %{users: Accounts.list_users()}} end)} end ### 14.2 PubSub.subscribe without connected? # BAD def mount(_, _, socket) do Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(MyApp.PubSub, "room") {:ok, socket} end # GOOD def mount(_, _, socket) do if connected?(socket), do: Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(MyApp.PubSub, "room") {:ok, socket} end ### 14.3 Routes.*_path leftovers # BAD <.link navigate={Routes.user_path(@socket, :show, user)}>View # GOOD <.link navigate={~p"/users/#{user}"}>View ### 14.4 Bare %Changeset{} passed to <.form> # BAD <.form :let={f} for={@changeset} phx-submit="save"> <.input field={f[:name]} /> # GOOD # In mount: assign(socket, :form, to_form(changeset)) <.form for={@form} phx-submit="save"> <.input field={@form[:name]} /> ### 14.5 Enum over assigns for lists >100 items # BAD def mount(_, _, socket), do: {:ok, assign(socket, :messages, Chat.list_messages())} # GOOD def mount(_, _, socket), do: {:ok, stream(socket, :messages, Chat.list_messages())} ### 14.6 assign_new for values needing fresh compute # BAD assign_new(socket, :now, fn -> DateTime.utc_now() end) # stale after reconnect # GOOD assign(socket, :now, DateTime.utc_now()) ### 14.7 Legacy navigation helpers # BAD <%= live_patch "Sort", to: Routes.user_path(@socket, :index, sort: :name) %> # GOOD <.link patch={~p"/users?sort=name"}>Sort ### 14.8 LiveComponent without :id # BAD <.live_component module={FilterPanel} users={@users} /> # GOOD <.live_component module={FilterPanel} id="filter-main" users={@users} /> ### 14.9 Repo calls inside function components # BAD def user_card(assigns) do user = Repo.get(User, assigns.user_id) ~H"
<%= user.name %>
" end # GOOD — parent passes the user in attr :user, User, required: true def user_card(assigns), do: ~H"
<%= @user.name %>
" ### 14.10 PubSub.broadcast called from handle_event # BAD def handle_event("save", params, socket) do {:ok, msg} = Repo.insert(Message.changeset(%Message{}, params)) Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast(MyApp.PubSub, "room:1", {:new, msg}) {:noreply, socket} end # GOOD — context owns the broadcast def handle_event("save", params, socket) do {:ok, _msg} = Chat.create_message(params) # broadcasts internally {:noreply, socket} end ### 14.11 Props assigned outside update/2 # BAD def handle_event("reload", _, socket) do {:noreply, assign(socket, :user, socket.assigns.user)} end # GOOD — props only flow through update/2 def handle_event("reload", _, socket) do send(self(), {:reload_user, socket.assigns.user.id}) {:noreply, socket} end ### 14.12 Raw params map stuffed into %Action{} # BAD def handle_event("save", params, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :save, payload: params}, socket) end # GOOD def handle_event("save", %{"message" => %{"body" => body}}, socket) do handle_action(%Action{type: :save, payload: %{body: body}}, socket) end ### 14.13 Re-fetching after on_mount scoped # BAD — on_mount already put the post in assigns def mount(%{"id" => id}, _, socket) do post = Posts.get_post!(id) # wasted query, TOCTOU risk {:ok, assign(socket, :post, post)} end # GOOD def mount(_, _, socket) do {:ok, socket} # socket.assigns.post already set by on_mount end ### 14.14 Template <%= for %> instead of :for= # BAD <%= for user <- @users do %>
